title: Bootstrap Core Infrastructure (Bolt - Kubernetes/Helm)
description: Provision the dev infrastructure foundation for Sigma-1: CloudNative-PG PostgreSQL 16 cluster with schemas (rms, finance, vetting, social, public, audit, crm), Valkey 7.2 via OpsTree Redis Operator, Cloudflare R2 bucket configuration, NATS subjects for fire-and-forget events, External Secrets for all third-party API keys, Cilium network policies for namespace isolation, Morgan workspace PVC, Signal-CLI sidecar resources, and the sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap aggregating all connection strings. Single-replica dev mode for all stateful services.
details:
1. Create namespace `sigma1` for backend services and `openclaw` for Morgan agent.
2. Deploy CloudNative-PG Cluster CR `sigma1-postgres` in `databases` namespace:
   - Single instance, 50Gi storage, database `sigma1`, owner `sigma1_user`.
   - Bootstrap initdb SQL to create schemas: `rms`, `finance`, `vetting`, `social`, `public`, `audit`, `crm`.
   - Create per-service PostgreSQL roles: `catalog_svc` (owns `public`), `rms_svc` (owns `rms`), `finance_vetting_svc` (owns `finance` + `vetting`), `social_svc` (owns `social`). All roles get INSERT on `audit` schema. No cross-schema SELECT grants.
3. Deploy Valkey 7.2 via existing OpsTree Redis Operator CR `sigma1-valkey` in `databases` namespace (image: `valkey/valkey:7.2-alpine`, single replica).
4. Configure Cloudflare R2 bucket `sigma1-media` via Cloudflare operator or Terraform. Create sub-prefixes: `products/`, `events/`, `social/`, `portfolio/`.
5. Configure NATS subjects: `sigma1.audit.>`, `sigma1.gdpr.>`, `sigma1.notifications.>` — fire-and-forget only.
6. Create ExternalSecret CRs for: `stripe-api-key`, `opencorporates-api-key`, `linkedin-api-key`, `google-reviews-api-key`, `credit-api-key`, `elevenlabs-api-key`, `twilio-credentials`, `openai-api-key`, `cloudflare-r2-credentials`, `google-calendar-credentials`, `instagram-api-token`, `facebook-api-token`, `tiktok-api-token`, `signal-cli-config`.
7. Create Cilium NetworkPolicy CRs:
   - `sigma1` namespace pods can reach `databases` namespace (PostgreSQL, Valkey).
   - `sigma1` namespace pods can reach each other (internal service calls).
   - `openclaw` namespace can reach `sigma1` namespace (Morgan → backend services).
   - Deny all other inter-namespace traffic.
8. Create PVC `morgan-workspace` (10Gi) in `openclaw` namespace.
9. Create ConfigMap `sigma1-infra-endpoints` in `sigma1` namespace with keys:
   - `POSTGRES_URL=postgresql://sigma1_user:<secret>@sigma1-postgres-rw.databases:5432/sigma1`
   - `VALKEY_URL=redis://sigma1-valkey.databases:6379`
   - `R2_ENDPOINT=https://<account>.r2.cloudflarestorage.com`
   - `R2_BUCKET=sigma1-media`
   - `NATS_URL=nats://nats.nats:4222`
10. Create ServiceMonitor CRs for Prometheus scraping of `/metrics` on all services (to be deployed in later tasks).
11. Create Cloudflare Tunnel CR for external ingress routing (rules to be finalized in Task 9).
test_strategy: 1. Verify `kubectl get cluster sigma1-postgres -n databases` shows status READY with 1/1 instances. 2. Connect to PostgreSQL and confirm all 7 schemas exist with correct ownership: `\dn+` shows rms owned by rms_svc, finance owned by finance_vetting_svc, etc. 3. Verify `kubectl get redis sigma1-valkey -n databases` shows Running; `redis-cli -h sigma1-valkey.databases PING` returns PONG. 4. Verify R2 bucket exists: `aws s3 ls s3://sigma1-media/ --endpoint-url $R2_ENDPOINT` succeeds. 5. Verify NATS subjects: `nats sub sigma1.audit.test` receives messages published to that subject. 6. Verify all ExternalSecret CRs show status SecretSynced. 7. Verify Cilium policies: a pod in `sigma1` can reach `databases` namespace; a pod in `default` cannot reach `sigma1`. 8. Verify ConfigMap `sigma1-infra-endpoints` exists with all expected keys. 9. Verify PVC `morgan-workspace` is Bound.

decision_points.question: Should Cloudflare R2 bucket provisioning use the Cloudflare Kubernetes Operator or Terraform? The operator keeps everything in-cluster but may have limited R2 support; Terraform is more mature for R2 but introduces an out-of-band dependency.
decision_points.context: Task details mention 'Cloudflare operator or Terraform' — this must be resolved before implementation.
options: Terraform module applied via CI pipeline
Manual bucket creation with only credential ExternalSecrets managed in-cluster

decision_points.question: Which External Secrets backend (secret store) should the ExternalSecret CRs reference? The manifests need a SecretStore or ClusterSecretStore CR pointing to the actual vault.
decision_points.context: 14 ExternalSecret CRs are specified but the backing provider (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, 1Password, etc.) is not specified.

decision_points.question: Is a shared NATS cluster already deployed in the `nats` namespace, or does this task need to provision one?
decision_points.context: The ConfigMap references `nats://nats.nats:4222` implying a pre-existing NATS deployment, but NATS subject configuration is listed as in-scope work.
options: NATS cluster already exists — only configure subjects/streams
Provision a new single-replica NATS server as part of this task

subtasks.title: Create Kubernetes namespaces (sigma1, openclaw, databases)
subtasks.description: Create the three namespaces required by all subsequent infrastructure resources: `sigma1` for backend services, `openclaw` for the Morgan agent workspace, and `databases` for stateful data stores (PostgreSQL, Valkey). Apply standard labels for Cilium policy selectors.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `namespaces.yaml` manifest with three Namespace resources:
   - `sigma1` with labels `app.kubernetes.io/part-of: sigma1`, `purpose: backend`
   - `openclaw` with labels `app.kubernetes.io/part-of: sigma1`, `purpose: agent`
   - `databases` with labels `app.kubernetes.io/part-of: sigma1`, `purpose: data` (if not already existing)
2. Add annotation `meta.helm.sh/release-name: sigma1-infra` for Helm tracking.
3. Apply with `kubectl apply -f namespaces.yaml`.
4. Ensure `databases` namespace creation is idempotent (it may already exist if operators are pre-installed).
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `kubectl get ns sigma1 openclaw databases` and verify all three exist with STATUS Active and correct labels visible via `kubectl get ns --show-labels`.

subtasks.title: Deploy CloudNative-PG Cluster CR for PostgreSQL 16
subtasks.description: Create the CloudNative-PG Cluster custom resource `sigma1-postgres` in the `databases` namespace with a single instance, 50Gi PVC, database `sigma1`, and owner role `sigma1_user`. This CR handles only the cluster lifecycle — schema/role init is a separate subtask.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `sigma1-postgres-cluster.yaml` with kind `Cluster` (apiVersion `postgresql.cnpg.io/v1`):
   - `metadata.name: sigma1-postgres`, `metadata.namespace: databases`
   - `spec.instances: 1`
   - `spec.storage.size: 50Gi`
   - `spec.postgresql.parameters`: tune `shared_buffers`, `work_mem` for dev
   - `spec.bootstrap.initdb.database: sigma1`
   - `spec.bootstrap.initdb.owner: sigma1_user`
   - `spec.bootstrap.initdb.postInitSQL` or `postInitApplicationSQL`: reference a ConfigMap containing the schema/role SQL (created in subtask 1003)
   - Image tag: PostgreSQL 16 (use CNPG default or pin `ghcr.io/cloudnative-pg/postgresql:16`)
2. Create a Secret `sigma1-postgres-superuser` with the superuser password (or rely on CNPG auto-generation).
3. Apply the manifest and wait for the cluster to become Ready.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `kubectl get cluster sigma1-postgres -n databases` and verify STATUS is `Cluster in healthy state` with 1/1 ready instances. Verify `kubectl get pods -n databases -l cnpg.io/cluster=sigma1-postgres` shows 1 running pod.

subtasks.title: Create PostgreSQL schema initialization SQL and role grants
subtasks.description: Write the SQL init script that creates all 7 schemas (rms, finance, vetting, social, public, audit, crm), the 4 per-service roles (catalog_svc, rms_svc, finance_vetting_svc, social_svc), schema ownership assignments, and audit INSERT grants. Package as a ConfigMap referenced by the CNPG postInitSQL hook.
subtasks.test_strategy: Connect to the PostgreSQL instance via `kubectl exec` and run `\dn+` to verify all 7 schemas exist with correct owners. Run `SELECT has_schema_privilege('catalog_svc', 'rms', 'USAGE')` which should return `false`. Run `SELECT has_schema_privilege('rms_svc', 'audit', 'USAGE')` which should return `true`. Verify `INSERT` on audit works for each role but `SELECT` on other schemas fails.

subtasks.title: Deploy Valkey 7.2 via OpsTree Redis Operator CR
subtasks.description: Create the OpsTree Redis Operator custom resource `sigma1-valkey` in the `databases` namespace using the Valkey 7.2 Alpine image with a single replica for dev mode.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `sigma1-valkey.yaml` with the OpsTree Redis CR:
   - `apiVersion: redis.redis.opstreelabs.in/v1beta2`, `kind: Redis` (standalone mode)
   - `metadata.name: sigma1-valkey`, `metadata.namespace: databases`
   - `spec.kubernetesConfig.image: valkey/valkey:7.2-alpine`
   - `spec.kubernetesConfig.resources`: set requests (128Mi/100m) and limits (256Mi/200m) for dev
   - `spec.storage.volumeClaimTemplate`: 5Gi PVC for persistence
   - Single replica (standalone mode, not cluster or sentinel)
2. Verify the operator creates the StatefulSet and Service `sigma1-valkey` in `databases` namespace.
3. Ensure the service DNS resolves as `sigma1-valkey.databases.svc.cluster.local:6379`.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `kubectl get redis sigma1-valkey -n databases` and verify status is Running. Run `kubectl exec` into a debug pod and execute `redis-cli -h sigma1-valkey.databases.svc.cluster.local PING` — expect `PONG`. Verify `INFO server` shows Valkey 7.2.

subtasks.title: Configure Cloudflare R2 bucket and sub-prefixes
subtasks.description: Provision the Cloudflare R2 bucket `sigma1-media` with the required sub-prefixes (products/, events/, social/, portfolio/) and generate R2 API credentials for application access.
subtasks.details:
1. Based on the resolved decision point (Cloudflare Operator vs Terraform vs manual):
   - **If Terraform**: Create a Terraform module `r2-bucket` with `cloudflare_r2_bucket` resource named `sigma1-media`. Create an R2 API token with read/write on the bucket. Output the endpoint URL, access key ID, and secret access key.
   - **If Operator**: Create the appropriate Cloudflare operator CR for R2 bucket provisioning.
   - **If Manual**: Document the manual creation steps and focus on storing credentials.
2. Create placeholder objects to establish prefixes: `products/.keep`, `events/.keep`, `social/.keep`, `portfolio/.keep` (R2 doesn't have true folders).
3. Store the R2 credentials in the external secrets backend so the ExternalSecret CR (subtask 1007) can sync them.
4. Record the R2 endpoint URL (format: `https://<account-id>.r2.cloudflarestorage.com`) for the ConfigMap.
subtasks.test_strategy: Verify bucket exists by running `aws s3 ls s3://sigma1-media/ --endpoint-url $R2_ENDPOINT` using the generated credentials. Verify all 4 prefix placeholder objects are listed. Verify the API token has read/write permissions by uploading and downloading a test object.

subtasks.title: Configure NATS subjects for fire-and-forget events
subtasks.description: Set up NATS subjects `sigma1.audit.>`, `sigma1.gdpr.>`, and `sigma1.notifications.>` for fire-and-forget (core NATS pub/sub) messaging. If NATS needs provisioning, deploy a single-replica NATS server.
subtasks.details:
1. Based on the resolved decision point (existing NATS vs new):
   - **If existing**: Verify connectivity to `nats://nats.nats:4222`.
   - **If new**: Deploy NATS via Helm chart `nats/nats` into `nats` namespace with single replica, no JetStream (fire-and-forget only), and basic auth disabled for dev.
2. Core NATS subjects are implicit (no pre-creation needed) — but document the subject hierarchy:
   - `sigma1.audit.>` — all audit log events
   - `sigma1.gdpr.>` — GDPR-related events (deletion requests, consent changes)
   - `sigma1.notifications.>` — notification dispatch events
3. Create a NATS account/user configuration if auth is enabled.
4. Test pub/sub on each subject wildcard to verify connectivity.
5. Document the subject naming convention for downstream service teams.
subtasks.test_strategy: From a test pod in `sigma1` namespace, run `nats pub sigma1.audit.test 'hello'` and in another terminal `nats sub 'sigma1.audit.>'` — verify the message is received. Repeat for `sigma1.gdpr.test` and `sigma1.notifications.test`. Verify `nats server info` shows a healthy server.

subtasks.title: Create ExternalSecret CRs for all 14 third-party API keys
subtasks.description: Create ExternalSecret custom resources for all 14 third-party credentials: stripe-api-key, opencorporates-api-key, linkedin-api-key, google-reviews-api-key, credit-api-key, elevenlabs-api-key, twilio-credentials, openai-api-key, cloudflare-r2-credentials, google-calendar-credentials, instagram-api-token, facebook-api-token, tiktok-api-token, signal-cli-config. Also create the ClusterSecretStore or SecretStore CR pointing to the external secrets backend.
subtasks.details:
1. Create a `SecretStore` (or `ClusterSecretStore`) CR in `sigma1` namespace pointing to the resolved external secrets backend (Vault, AWS SM, etc.).
2. Create 14 individual ExternalSecret manifests in `sigma1` namespace, each mapping a remote secret path to a local Kubernetes Secret:
   - `stripe-api-key` → Secret with key `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`
   - `opencorporates-api-key` → Secret with key `OPENCORPORATES_API_KEY`
   - `linkedin-api-key` → Secret with key `LINKEDIN_API_KEY`
   - `google-reviews-api-key` → Secret with key `GOOGLE_REVIEWS_API_KEY`
   - `credit-api-key` → Secret with key `CREDIT_API_KEY`
   - `elevenlabs-api-key` → Secret with key `ELEVENLABS_API_KEY`
   - `twilio-credentials` → Secret with keys `TWILIO_ACCOUNT_SID`, `TWILIO_AUTH_TOKEN`, `TWILIO_PHONE_NUMBER`
   - `openai-api-key` → Secret with key `OPENAI_API_KEY`
   - `cloudflare-r2-credentials` → Secret with keys `R2_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `R2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`
   - `google-calendar-credentials` → Secret with keys `GOOGLE_CALENDAR_CLIENT_ID`, `GOOGLE_CALENDAR_CLIENT_SECRET`
   - `instagram-api-token` → Secret with key `INSTAGRAM_API_TOKEN`
   - `facebook-api-token` → Secret with key `FACEBOOK_API_TOKEN`
   - `tiktok-api-token` → Secret with key `TIKTOK_API_TOKEN`
   - `signal-cli-config` → Secret with key `SIGNAL_CLI_CONFIG` (may be a multi-line config file)
3. Set `refreshInterval: 1h` on all ExternalSecrets.
4. Use consistent naming: ExternalSecret name matches the target Secret name.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `kubectl get externalsecrets -n sigma1` and verify all 14 show `SecretSynced` status. Run `kubectl get secrets -n sigma1` and verify corresponding Secrets exist. Spot-check 2-3 secrets: `kubectl get secret stripe-api-key -n sigma1 -o jsonpath='{.data.STRIPE_SECRET_KEY}'` should return a non-empty base64 value.

subtasks.title: Create Cilium NetworkPolicy CRs for namespace isolation
subtasks.description: Define Cilium CiliumNetworkPolicy CRs that enforce: sigma1→databases allowed, sigma1 intra-namespace allowed, openclaw→sigma1 allowed, and deny-all for other inter-namespace traffic.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `cilium-netpol-sigma1-to-databases.yaml`:
   - CiliumNetworkPolicy in `sigma1` namespace
   - Egress rule allowing traffic from any pod in `sigma1` to pods in `databases` namespace on ports 5432 (PostgreSQL) and 6379 (Valkey)
   - Use `toEndpoints` with `matchLabels` on namespace label `purpose: data`
2. Create `cilium-netpol-sigma1-internal.yaml`:
   - CiliumNetworkPolicy in `sigma1` namespace
   - Allow all ingress/egress between pods within `sigma1` namespace
3. Create `cilium-netpol-openclaw-to-sigma1.yaml`:
   - CiliumNetworkPolicy in `openclaw` namespace
   - Egress rule allowing traffic from `openclaw` pods to `sigma1` namespace pods
   - Corresponding ingress rule in `sigma1` namespace allowing traffic from `openclaw`
4. Create `cilium-netpol-default-deny.yaml`:
   - CiliumClusterWideNetworkPolicy or per-namespace default deny for `sigma1` and `openclaw`
   - Default deny all ingress and egress, with explicit allows from above policies taking precedence
5. Allow DNS egress (port 53 to kube-dns) in all policies.
6. Allow egress to external (0.0.0.0/0) from `sigma1` for third-party API calls.
subtasks.test_strategy: 1. Deploy a test pod in `sigma1` and verify it can `curl sigma1-postgres-rw.databases:5432` (TCP connect succeeds). 2. Deploy a test pod in `default` namespace and verify it CANNOT reach pods in `sigma1` (connection timeout/refused). 3. Deploy a test pod in `openclaw` and verify it can reach a service in `sigma1`. 4. Verify DNS resolution works from all namespaces (`nslookup kubernetes.default`). 5. Run `kubectl get ciliumnetworkpolicies -A` and verify all policies are applied.

subtasks.title: Create Morgan workspace PVC in openclaw namespace
subtasks.description: Create a PersistentVolumeClaim `morgan-workspace` with 10Gi capacity in the `openclaw` namespace for the Morgan agent's persistent workspace storage.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `morgan-workspace-pvc.yaml`:
   ```yaml
   apiVersion: v1
   kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
   metadata:
     name: morgan-workspace
     namespace: openclaw
     labels:
       app.kubernetes.io/part-of: sigma1
       app.kubernetes.io/component: morgan-agent
   spec:
     accessModes:
       - ReadWriteOnce
     resources:
       requests:
         storage: 10Gi
     # Use default StorageClass unless a specific one is required
   ```
2. Apply the manifest.
3. Verify the PVC transitions to Bound state (may be Pending until a pod mounts it, depending on StorageClass `volumeBindingMode`).
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `kubectl get pvc morgan-workspace -n openclaw` and verify it exists with STATUS `Bound` (or `Pending` with `WaitForFirstConsumer` binding mode — in that case, create a temporary pod that mounts it and verify it transitions to Bound).

subtasks.title: Create sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap aggregating all connection strings
subtasks.description: Create the ConfigMap `sigma1-infra-endpoints` in the `sigma1` namespace containing connection strings for PostgreSQL, Valkey, R2, and NATS. This ConfigMap will be consumed via `envFrom` by all backend services.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `sigma1-infra-endpoints-configmap.yaml`:
   ```yaml
   apiVersion: v1
   kind: ConfigMap
   metadata:
     name: sigma1-infra-endpoints
     namespace: sigma1
     labels:
       app.kubernetes.io/part-of: sigma1
       app.kubernetes.io/component: config
   data:
     POSTGRES_HOST: sigma1-postgres-rw.databases.svc.cluster.local
     POSTGRES_PORT: "5432"
     POSTGRES_DB: sigma1
     POSTGRES_USER: sigma1_user
     VALKEY_URL: redis://sigma1-valkey.databases.svc.cluster.local:6379
     R2_ENDPOINT: "https://<account-id>.r2.cloudflarestorage.com"
     R2_BUCKET: sigma1-media
     NATS_URL: nats://nats.nats.svc.cluster.local:4222
   ```
2. Note: `POSTGRES_URL` is intentionally split into components so the password can come from a Secret reference rather than being in the ConfigMap. Services should construct the full URL or use individual env vars.
3. Replace `<account-id>` with the actual Cloudflare account ID from the R2 setup.
4. Apply the manifest.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `kubectl get configmap sigma1-infra-endpoints -n sigma1 -o yaml` and verify all expected keys are present with correct values. From a test pod with `envFrom: configMapRef: sigma1-infra-endpoints`, verify `echo $VALKEY_URL` outputs the correct Redis URL and `echo $NATS_URL` outputs the correct NATS URL.

subtasks.title: Create ServiceMonitor CRs for Prometheus metrics scraping
subtasks.description: Create ServiceMonitor custom resources for Prometheus Operator to discover and scrape /metrics endpoints on all Sigma-1 backend services as they are deployed in subsequent tasks.
subtasks.details:
1. Create a set of ServiceMonitor CRs in `sigma1` namespace that use label selectors to auto-discover services:
   - `sigma1-services-monitor.yaml`: matches all services with label `app.kubernetes.io/part-of: sigma1` and `metrics: enabled`
   - `spec.endpoints[0].port: metrics` (or `http` if metrics are on the main port)
   - `spec.endpoints[0].path: /metrics`
   - `spec.endpoints[0].interval: 30s`
   - `spec.namespaceSelector.matchNames: [sigma1]`
2. Create a ServiceMonitor for PostgreSQL metrics (CNPG exports metrics natively):
   - Target `sigma1-postgres-metrics` service in `databases` namespace
3. Create a ServiceMonitor for Valkey metrics:
   - Target `sigma1-valkey-metrics` in `databases` namespace (OpsTree operator may expose this)
4. Ensure the Prometheus Operator's `serviceMonitorSelector` matches these monitors (check existing Prometheus CR config).
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `kubectl get servicemonitors -n sigma1` and `kubectl get servicemonitors -n databases` — verify all monitors exist. Check Prometheus targets UI (port-forward to Prometheus) and verify `sigma1-postgres` appears as a target (even if other services aren't deployed yet, the CNPG target should be active).

subtasks.title: Create Cloudflare Tunnel CR for external ingress
subtasks.description: Create the Cloudflare Tunnel custom resource for external ingress routing into the cluster. Configure the tunnel with placeholder routing rules to be finalized when services are deployed.
subtasks.details:
1. Create the Cloudflare Tunnel CR (or Deployment + ConfigMap if using cloudflared directly):
   - If using cloudflare-operator: Create a `Tunnel` CR with the tunnel name `sigma1-tunnel`
   - If using cloudflared directly: Create a Deployment of `cloudflare/cloudflared` with a ConfigMap for ingress rules
2. Configure the tunnel credential secret (tunnel token or credential file) — reference from ExternalSecrets or create a dedicated secret.
3. Add placeholder ingress rules in the tunnel configuration:
   ```yaml
   ingress:
     - hostname: api.sigma1.example.com
       service: http://placeholder.sigma1.svc.cluster.local:8080
     - hostname: app.sigma1.example.com
       service: http://placeholder.sigma1.svc.cluster.local:3000
     - service: http_status:404  # catch-all
   ```
4. These rules will be updated in Task 9 (production hardening) with actual service endpoints.
5. Deploy the tunnel in the `sigma1` namespace.
subtasks.test_strategy: Verify the cloudflared pod is running: `kubectl get pods -n sigma1 -l app=cloudflared`. Check tunnel status via Cloudflare dashboard or `cloudflared tunnel info sigma1-tunnel` — it should show as CONNECTED. Verify the catch-all rule returns 404 for unknown hostnames.

subtasks.title: Validate end-to-end infrastructure integration
subtasks.description: Run a comprehensive integration validation that verifies all infrastructure components are functional, interconnected, and accessible from the sigma1 namespace using the ConfigMap and secrets.
subtasks.details:
1. Deploy a temporary validation pod in `sigma1` namespace with:
   - `envFrom: configMapRef: sigma1-infra-endpoints`
   - Mounted secrets for PostgreSQL password and R2 credentials
   - Tools: `psql`, `redis-cli`, `nats`, `curl`, `aws-cli`
2. Run validation script:
   a. PostgreSQL: `psql $POSTGRES_URL -c '\dn+'` — verify 7 schemas with correct ownership
   b. PostgreSQL roles: Connect as each service role and verify schema access/isolation
   c. Valkey: `redis-cli -u $VALKEY_URL PING` — expect PONG
   d. R2: `aws s3 ls s3://$R2_BUCKET/ --endpoint-url $R2_ENDPOINT` — expect prefix listings
   e. NATS: Pub/sub test on each subject wildcard
   f. Network policies: Verify pod-to-pod connectivity per Cilium rules
   g. Secrets: Verify all 14 ExternalSecrets show SecretSynced
3. Clean up the validation pod after tests pass.
4. Generate a summary report of all checks.
subtasks.test_strategy: All validation checks in the script must pass with zero failures. The validation pod should exit with code 0. Capture and review the summary report showing green status for: PostgreSQL connectivity, schema existence, role isolation, Valkey PONG, R2 bucket access, NATS pub/sub, network policy enforcement, all 14 secrets synced, PVC bound, ConfigMap values correct.

title: Build Equipment Catalog Service (Rex - Rust/Axum)
description: Implement the high-performance Equipment Catalog REST API serving 533+ products across 24 categories with real-time availability checking, barcode/SKU lookup, image URL management (Cloudflare R2), machine-readable equipment API for AI agents, rate limiting via Valkey, and Prometheus metrics. Owns the `public` schema in PostgreSQL.
details:
1. Initialize Rust workspace with Cargo.toml: dependencies include `axum 0.7`, `sqlx` (PostgreSQL), `serde`, `uuid`, `chrono`, `rust_decimal`, `tokio`, `tower-http` (CORS, compression, tracing), `redis` (Valkey), `aws-sdk-s3` (R2), `prometheus` crate.
2. Define data models matching PRD: `Product` (id, name, category_id, description, day_rate as Decimal, weight_kg, dimensions as JSONB, image_urls as Vec<String> storing R2 URLs + content_hash + width/height per D13, specs as JSONB, created_at), `Category` (id, name, parent_id, icon, sort_order), `Availability` (product_id, date_from, date_to, quantity_available, reserved, booked).
3. Write SQLx migrations for `public` schema: `categories` table, `products` table with GIN index on `specs`, `product_images` table (product_id, url, content_hash, width, height, sort_order), `availability` table with composite index on (product_id, date_from, date_to), `bookings` table for reservation tracking.
4. Implement endpoints:
   - `GET /api/v1/catalog/categories` — list all categories with parent hierarchy.
   - `GET /api/v1/catalog/products` — paginated, filterable by category_id, search term (full-text on name+description), price range, availability date range. Use query params.
   - `GET /api/v1/catalog/products/:id` — full product detail with images and specs.
   - `GET /api/v1/catalog/products/:id/availability?from=&to=` — compute available quantity for date range. Must respond in <500ms.
   - `POST /api/v1/catalog/products` — admin-only (allowlist middleware per D11: check X-Service-Identity header for Morgan or X-User-ID for Mike). Validate with serde.
   - `PATCH /api/v1/catalog/products/:id` — admin-only, partial update.
   - `GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog` — machine-readable JSON (structured for AI agent consumption, includes all products with simplified schema).
   - `POST /api/v1/equipment-api/checkout` — programmatic booking: reserve items for date range, decrement availability.
   - `DELETE /api/v1/gdpr/customer/{id}` — delete all customer-related data from public schema (per D15).
   - `GET /metrics` — Prometheus metrics (request count, latency histogram, error rate).
   - `GET /health/live` — return 200 if process alive.
   - `GET /health/ready` — return 200 if PostgreSQL and Valkey connections healthy.
5. Implement rate limiting middleware using Valkey: sliding window per IP, 100 req/min for public endpoints, 1000 req/min for authenticated.
6. Implement Valkey caching: cache category list (TTL 5min), product detail (TTL 1min), availability results (TTL 30s).
7. Admin allowlist middleware: extract X-Service-Identity or X-User-ID header, check against allowlist (morgan-service, mike-user-id from env). Return 403 if not matched. Design as Tower middleware layer for future RBAC swap (per D11).
8. Dockerfile: multi-stage build (rust:1.75-slim for build, debian:bookworm-slim for runtime). Binary name: `equipment-catalog`.
9. Kubernetes Deployment manifest: namespace `sigma1`, 2 replicas, envFrom `sigma1-infra-endpoints` ConfigMap, resource limits (256Mi/500m request, 512Mi/1000m limit), liveness/readiness probes on health endpoints.
10. Seed data script: load 24 categories and 533+ products from a JSON/CSV fixture file.
test_strategy: 1. Unit tests for availability calculation logic: given 10 units, 3 reserved, 2 booked for overlapping dates, verify quantity_available=5. 2. Integration test: POST a product (admin), GET it back, verify all fields match. 3. Integration test: check availability for a date range with no bookings returns full quantity; after checkout, availability decreases. 4. Verify rate limiting: send 101 requests in 1 minute from same IP, confirm 429 on request 101. 5. Verify admin allowlist: request to POST /api/v1/catalog/products without allowlisted header returns 403; with header returns 201. 6. Verify /health/ready returns 200 when DB is up, 503 when DB connection is dropped. 7. Verify /metrics endpoint returns Prometheus-format text with request_duration_seconds histogram. 8. Verify availability endpoint responds in <500ms with 533 products loaded (benchmark test). 9. Verify GDPR deletion endpoint removes all data for a given customer ID and returns 200. 10. Verify machine-readable /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog returns valid JSON with all products.
decision_points: Full-text search strategy: use PostgreSQL tsvector/GIN natively via SQLx or integrate a separate search engine? tsvector is simpler for v1 but may limit ranking flexibility.

Availability data model: store per-day inventory rows vs. date-range rows with overlap detection? Per-day is simpler to query but generates more rows; range-based is storage-efficient but requires complex overlap logic.

GDPR deletion scope: which tables in the public schema contain customer-related data? Need to clarify whether bookings reference a customer_id and whether product reviews or other customer-linked entities exist.
Seed data format: JSON fixture vs. CSV? JSON preserves nested structures (specs JSONB, image arrays) more naturally but may be harder to maintain for 533+ products.

subtasks.title: Scaffold Rust workspace, Cargo dependencies, and application entrypoint
subtasks.description: Initialize the Rust project with Cargo.toml, configure all required dependencies (axum 0.7, sqlx, serde, uuid, chrono, rust_decimal, tokio, tower-http, redis, aws-sdk-s3, prometheus), set up the main.rs entrypoint with Axum router skeleton, shared AppState struct (holding DB pool, Valkey connection, config), and tower-http layers for CORS, compression, and tracing.
subtasks.details:
1. Run `cargo init --name equipment-catalog` in the service directory.
2. Add all dependencies to Cargo.toml with exact versions: axum = "0.7", axum-extra (for query extraction), sqlx = { version = "0.7", features = ["runtime-tokio", "postgres", "uuid", "chrono", "rust_decimal", "json"] }, serde = { features = ["derive"] }, serde_json, uuid = { features = ["v4", "serde"] }, chrono = { features = ["serde"] }, rust_decimal = { features = ["serde-with-str"] }, tokio = { features = ["full"] }, tower-http = { features = ["cors", "compression-gzip", "trace"] }, redis = { features = ["tokio-comp"] }, aws-sdk-s3, prometheus, tracing, tracing-subscriber.
3. Create `src/main.rs` with tokio::main, read config from env vars (DATABASE_URL, VALKEY_URL, R2_ENDPOINT, R2_BUCKET, ALLOWED_SERVICE_IDENTITIES, ALLOWED_USER_IDS, LISTEN_ADDR defaulting to 0.0.0.0:8080).
4. Define `AppState` struct in `src/state.rs`: sqlx::PgPool, redis::Client, aws_sdk_s3::Client, PrometheusMetrics handle, config struct.
5. Create `src/router.rs` with placeholder route groups: `/api/v1/catalog`, `/api/v1/equipment-api`, `/api/v1/gdpr`, `/metrics`, `/health`.
6. Wire tower-http CORS (permissive for dev), gzip compression, and tracing layers onto the router.
7. Ensure `cargo build` succeeds and `cargo run` starts listening on the configured port.
subtasks.test_strategy: Verify `cargo build` completes without errors. Run the binary and confirm it binds to the configured port. Send a request to any route and confirm a 404 (not a connection error), proving the server is running.

subtasks.title: Define Rust data models and serde types for all domain entities
subtasks.description: Create Rust structs for Product, Category, ProductImage, Availability, Booking, and all API request/response DTOs with serde Serialize/Deserialize, sqlx::FromRow, and proper type mappings (Decimal for money, JSONB for specs/dimensions, Vec<String> for image URLs).
subtasks.details:
1. Create `src/models/category.rs`: `Category` struct with fields id (Uuid), name (String), parent_id (Option<Uuid>), icon (Option<String>), sort_order (i32). Derive sqlx::FromRow, Serialize, Deserialize.
2. Create `src/models/product.rs`: `Product` struct with id (Uuid), name (String), category_id (Uuid), description (String), day_rate (rust_decimal::Decimal), weight_kg (Option<f64>), dimensions (Option<serde_json::Value>), specs (Option<serde_json::Value>), created_at (chrono::DateTime<Utc>). Derive sqlx::FromRow, Serialize, Deserialize.
3. Create `src/models/product_image.rs`: `ProductImage` struct with id (Uuid), product_id (Uuid), url (String), content_hash (String), width (i32), height (i32), sort_order (i32).
4. Create `src/models/availability.rs`: `Availability` struct with product_id (Uuid), date_from (NaiveDate), date_to (NaiveDate), quantity_available (i32), reserved (i32), booked (i32).
5. Create `src/models/booking.rs`: `Booking` struct with id (Uuid), product_id (Uuid), customer_id (Uuid), date_from (NaiveDate), date_to (NaiveDate), quantity (i32), status (String), created_at (DateTime<Utc>).
6. Create `src/dto/` directory with request/response types: `CreateProductRequest`, `UpdateProductRequest` (all fields optional), `ProductListQuery` (category_id, search, price_min, price_max, available_from, available_to, page, per_page), `ProductResponse` (product + images), `AvailabilityQuery` (from, to), `AvailabilityResponse`, `CheckoutRequest` (product_id, customer_id, date_from, date_to, quantity), `CheckoutResponse`.
7. Create `src/models/mod.rs` re-exporting everything.
8. Ensure all structs compile and serde round-trip correctly.
subtasks.test_strategy: Write unit tests that serialize each struct to JSON and deserialize back, asserting field equality. Test edge cases: Decimal precision for day_rate, Option<Uuid> for parent_id, empty JSONB specs.

subtasks.title: Write SQLx migrations for public schema tables and indexes
subtasks.description: Create SQLx migration files for the categories, products, product_images, availability, and bookings tables in the public schema, including GIN index on products.specs, composite index on availability(product_id, date_from, date_to), full-text search index on products(name, description), and foreign key constraints.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `migrations/` directory at project root.
2. Migration 001: Create `categories` table — id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, parent_id UUID REFERENCES categories(id) ON DELETE SET NULL, icon VARCHAR(255), sort_order INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(). Add index on parent_id.
3. Migration 002: Create `products` table — id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), name VARCHAR(500) NOT NULL, category_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES categories(id), description TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '', day_rate DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL, weight_kg DOUBLE PRECISION, dimensions JSONB, specs JSONB, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(). Add GIN index on specs. Add full-text search column: `search_vector TSVECTOR GENERATED ALWAYS AS (to_tsvector('english', coalesce(name,'') || ' ' || coalesce(description,''))) STORED`. Add GIN index on search_vector.
4. Migration 003: Create `product_images` table — id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), product_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES products(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, url TEXT NOT NULL, content_hash VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL, width INTEGER NOT NULL, height INTEGER NOT NULL, sort_order INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0. Index on product_id.
5. Migration 004: Create `availability` table — id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), product_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES products(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, date_from DATE NOT NULL, date_to DATE NOT NULL, quantity_available INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0, reserved INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0, booked INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0, CHECK (date_from <= date_to). Composite index on (product_id, date_from, date_to).
6. Migration 005: Create `bookings` table — id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), product_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES products(id), customer_id UUID NOT NULL, date_from DATE NOT NULL, date_to DATE NOT NULL, quantity INTEGER NOT NULL, status VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'confirmed', created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(). Index on customer_id. Index on (product_id, date_from, date_to).
7. Verify migrations run successfully with `sqlx migrate run` against a test PostgreSQL instance.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `sqlx migrate run` against a clean PostgreSQL database and verify all tables, indexes, and constraints are created. Run `sqlx migrate run` again to confirm idempotency. Verify foreign key constraints by attempting to insert a product with a non-existent category_id (should fail).

subtasks.title: Implement category CRUD endpoints
subtasks.description: Implement GET /api/v1/catalog/categories endpoint returning all categories with parent hierarchy, including the hierarchical nesting logic to return a tree structure.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `src/handlers/categories.rs`.
2. Implement `list_categories` handler: query all categories from DB ordered by sort_order, build a nested tree structure in Rust (parent → children), return as JSON array where top-level categories contain a `children` array.
3. SQL query: `SELECT id, name, parent_id, icon, sort_order FROM categories ORDER BY sort_order ASC`.
4. Tree-building logic: collect all categories into a HashMap<Uuid, Category>, iterate to assign children to parents, return only root-level (parent_id IS NULL) categories with nested children.
5. Register route in router: `GET /api/v1/catalog/categories` → `list_categories`.
6. Return appropriate error responses (500 on DB error) with structured JSON error body { "error": "...", "code": "..." }.
subtasks.test_strategy: Integration test: seed 3 top-level categories and 5 subcategories, call GET /api/v1/catalog/categories, verify response is a tree with correct nesting. Verify sort_order is respected. Verify empty database returns empty array.

subtasks.title: Implement product listing endpoint with pagination, filtering, and full-text search
subtasks.description: Implement GET /api/v1/catalog/products with query parameters for category_id, search term (full-text on name+description), price_min, price_max, available_from, available_to, page, and per_page. Return paginated results with total count.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `src/handlers/products.rs`.
2. Implement `list_products` handler accepting `ProductListQuery` extracted via axum Query extractor.
3. Build dynamic SQL query using sqlx: base SELECT from products LEFT JOIN product_images. Conditionally append WHERE clauses:
   - `category_id`: WHERE category_id = $N
   - `search`: WHERE search_vector @@ plainto_tsquery('english', $N). Order by ts_rank if search is present.
   - `price_min`/`price_max`: WHERE day_rate >= $N AND day_rate <= $N
   - `available_from`/`available_to`: JOIN availability and filter WHERE quantity_available - reserved - booked > 0 for overlapping date range.
4. Pagination: default page=1, per_page=20 (max 100). Use LIMIT/OFFSET. Execute a parallel COUNT(*) query for total.
5. Return JSON: { "data": [...], "pagination": { "page": 1, "per_page": 20, "total": 533, "total_pages": 27 } }.
6. Each product in the list should include a subset of fields (id, name, category_id, day_rate, weight_kg, first image URL as thumbnail).
7. Register route: `GET /api/v1/catalog/products` → `list_products`.
8. Use sqlx::query_as for type-safe mapping. Handle edge cases: invalid page numbers, negative prices.
subtasks.test_strategy: Integration test: seed 30 products across 3 categories. Verify pagination returns 20 items on page 1, 10 on page 2. Verify category_id filter returns only matching products. Verify search term 'excavator' returns products with that word in name or description. Verify price range filter works. Verify total count is correct in pagination metadata.

subtasks.title: Implement product detail and admin create/update endpoints
subtasks.description: Implement GET /api/v1/catalog/products/:id for full product detail with images and specs, POST /api/v1/catalog/products for admin product creation, and PATCH /api/v1/catalog/products/:id for admin partial updates.
subtasks.details:
1. In `src/handlers/products.rs`, implement `get_product` handler: query product by UUID, join product_images ordered by sort_order, return full ProductResponse with all fields including images array.
2. Return 404 with { "error": "Product not found", "code": "NOT_FOUND" } if UUID doesn't match.
3. Implement `create_product` handler: accept CreateProductRequest (name, category_id, description, day_rate required; weight_kg, dimensions, specs optional). Validate category_id exists. INSERT into products, return 201 with created product.
4. Implement `update_product` handler: accept UpdateProductRequest where all fields are Option. Build dynamic UPDATE query setting only non-None fields. Return updated product. Return 404 if product doesn't exist.
5. For create/update, validate day_rate > 0, name not empty, category exists.
6. Register routes:
   - `GET /api/v1/catalog/products/:id` → `get_product` (public)
   - `POST /api/v1/catalog/products` → `create_product` (admin-protected, will be guarded in middleware subtask)
   - `PATCH /api/v1/catalog/products/:id` → `update_product` (admin-protected)
7. Return structured validation errors: { "error": "Validation failed", "code": "VALIDATION_ERROR", "details": [...] }.
subtasks.test_strategy: Integration test: POST a new product with all fields, verify 201 and returned body matches. GET the product by ID, verify all fields including images. PATCH the product changing only day_rate, verify only that field changed. Verify 404 for non-existent UUID. Verify validation errors for missing required fields or negative day_rate.

subtasks.title: Implement availability checking endpoint with <500ms performance target
subtasks.description: Implement GET /api/v1/catalog/products/:id/availability?from=&to= to compute available quantity for a given date range, ensuring the query responds in under 500ms even with 533+ products loaded.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `src/handlers/availability.rs`.
2. Implement `check_availability` handler: extract product_id from path, from/to NaiveDate from query params.
3. Validate: from <= to, from >= today, product exists (404 if not).
4. SQL query: SELECT quantity_available, reserved, booked FROM availability WHERE product_id = $1 AND date_from <= $2 AND date_to >= $3. For overlapping ranges, compute the minimum (quantity_available - reserved - booked) across all overlapping availability records.
5. If no availability records exist for the date range, return quantity 0.
6. Response: { "product_id": "...", "date_from": "...", "date_to": "...", "available_quantity": N }.
7. Add timing instrumentation: measure query execution time, log if >200ms as a warning.
8. Ensure the composite index on (product_id, date_from, date_to) is being used — add EXPLAIN ANALYZE in tests.
9. Register route: `GET /api/v1/catalog/products/:id/availability` → `check_availability`.
10. Handle edge cases: single-day range, far-future dates with no availability records.
subtasks.test_strategy: Unit test: given availability records [(day1-day5, qty=10, reserved=3, booked=2), (day3-day7, qty=8, reserved=1, booked=1)], querying day3-day5 returns min(10-3-2, 8-1-1) = 5. Integration test: seed availability data, query endpoint, verify correct available_quantity. Benchmark test: load 533 products with availability data, query 100 random products, assert all responses complete in <500ms (p99).

subtasks.title: Implement checkout/booking endpoint with availability decrement
subtasks.description: Implement POST /api/v1/equipment-api/checkout for programmatic booking: reserve items for a date range, atomically decrement availability, and create a booking record within a database transaction.
subtasks.details:
1. In `src/handlers/checkout.rs`, implement `checkout` handler accepting CheckoutRequest: { product_id, customer_id, date_from, date_to, quantity }.
2. Validate inputs: quantity > 0, date_from <= date_to, date_from >= today.
3. Use a database transaction (sqlx::PgPool::begin):
   a. SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on availability rows overlapping the date range to acquire row locks.
   b. Verify available quantity (quantity_available - reserved - booked) >= requested quantity for all overlapping periods. If not, rollback and return 409 Conflict with { "error": "Insufficient availability", "available": N }.
   c. UPDATE availability SET booked = booked + $quantity for overlapping rows.
   d. INSERT into bookings (product_id, customer_id, date_from, date_to, quantity, status='confirmed').
   e. Commit transaction.
4. Return 201 with CheckoutResponse: { "booking_id": "...", "product_id": "...", "customer_id": "...", "date_from": "...", "date_to": "...", "quantity": N, "status": "confirmed" }.
5. Handle concurrent requests via SELECT FOR UPDATE to prevent double-booking.
6. Register route: `POST /api/v1/equipment-api/checkout` → `checkout`.
subtasks.test_strategy: Integration test: seed product with availability (qty=5), checkout 3 units, verify availability decreases to 2. Attempt to checkout 3 more, verify 409 Conflict. Concurrency test: fire 5 simultaneous checkouts for 1 unit each on a product with qty=5, verify exactly 5 succeed and availability reaches 0.

subtasks.title: Implement admin allowlist Tower middleware
subtasks.description: Create a Tower middleware layer that extracts X-Service-Identity or X-User-ID headers and checks them against an environment-configured allowlist. Returns 403 Forbidden for unauthorized requests. Designed for easy future replacement with full RBAC.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `src/middleware/admin_auth.rs`.
2. Implement as a Tower Layer + Service. The middleware checks:
   a. If `X-Service-Identity` header is present and its value is in the ALLOWED_SERVICE_IDENTITIES env var (comma-separated list, e.g., 'morgan-service').
   b. Else if `X-User-ID` header is present and its value is in the ALLOWED_USER_IDS env var (comma-separated list, e.g., 'mike-user-id').
   c. If neither matches, return 403 with { "error": "Forbidden", "code": "FORBIDDEN" }.
3. Store the parsed allowlists in AppState so they're loaded once at startup.
4. Implement as `AdminAuthLayer` that can be applied to specific route groups: `.route_layer(AdminAuthLayer::new(state.clone()))`.
5. Apply to POST /api/v1/catalog/products and PATCH /api/v1/catalog/products/:id route group.
6. Add trait boundary documentation explaining how to swap this for a JWT/RBAC middleware in the future.
7. Log auth decisions at DEBUG level (who was allowed/denied).
subtasks.test_strategy: Unit test: construct middleware with allowlist ['morgan-service'], send request with X-Service-Identity: morgan-service, verify it passes through. Send without header, verify 403. Send with wrong value, verify 403. Integration test: POST /api/v1/catalog/products without auth header returns 403, with correct X-Service-Identity returns 201.

subtasks.title: Implement rate limiting middleware via Valkey sliding window
subtasks.description: Create a Tower middleware that enforces sliding window rate limiting using Valkey (Redis-compatible), with 100 req/min for public endpoints and 1000 req/min for authenticated requests.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `src/middleware/rate_limit.rs`.
2. Implement sliding window rate limiting using Valkey sorted sets:
   a. Key: `rate_limit:{ip}` for public, `rate_limit:{user_id}` for authenticated.
   b. On each request: ZADD key current_timestamp current_timestamp, ZREMRANGEBYSCORE key 0 (now - 60s), ZCARD key.
   c. If count > limit, return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header.
3. Determine if request is authenticated by checking if admin auth middleware already identified a user (look for an extension set by AdminAuthLayer).
4. Apply different limits: 100/min for unauthenticated, 1000/min for authenticated.
5. Implement as `RateLimitLayer` configurable with (valkey_client, public_limit, auth_limit).
6. Apply as a global layer on the router (before auth middleware).
7. Use redis async commands via the `redis` crate's tokio-compatible connection.
8. Handle Valkey connection failures gracefully: if Valkey is down, allow requests through (fail-open) but log a warning.
9. Add X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset response headers.
subtasks.test_strategy: Integration test with Valkey: send 100 requests from the same IP in rapid succession, verify all succeed. Send request 101, verify 429 status with Retry-After header. Verify X-RateLimit-Remaining decrements correctly. Test fail-open: stop Valkey, send request, verify it succeeds (not 500).

subtasks.title: Implement Valkey caching for categories, product details, and availability
subtasks.description: Add Valkey-based caching layer for category list (TTL 5min), product detail (TTL 1min), and availability results (TTL 30s), with cache invalidation on writes.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `src/cache.rs` with a `CacheService` struct wrapping the redis::Client.
2. Implement generic get/set methods: `async fn get<T: DeserializeOwned>(&self, key: &str) -> Option<T>` and `async fn set<T: Serialize>(&self, key: &str, value: &T, ttl_secs: u64)`.
3. Cache keys:
   - Categories list: `cache:categories:all` (TTL 300s)
   - Product detail: `cache:product:{id}` (TTL 60s)
   - Availability: `cache:availability:{product_id}:{from}:{to}` (TTL 30s)
4. Modify `list_categories` handler: check cache first, on miss query DB and populate cache.
5. Modify `get_product` handler: check cache first, on miss query DB and populate cache.
6. Modify `check_availability` handler: check cache first, on miss query DB and populate cache.
7. Cache invalidation:
   - On product create/update: delete `cache:product:{id}` and `cache:categories:all` (category counts might change).
   - On checkout: delete `cache:availability:{product_id}:*` using SCAN + DEL pattern.
8. Handle Valkey connection failures gracefully: on cache miss due to connection error, proceed to DB (fail-open).
9. Serialize cached values as JSON strings in Valkey.
subtasks.test_strategy: Integration test: query GET /api/v1/catalog/categories twice, verify second request is faster (cache hit). Verify after cache TTL expires, a fresh DB query is made. Verify that after creating a product, the product cache key is invalidated. Verify availability cache is invalidated after a checkout.

subtasks.title: Implement machine-readable equipment API endpoint for AI agents
subtasks.description: Implement GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog returning a simplified, machine-readable JSON catalog of all products structured for AI agent consumption.
subtasks.details:
1. In `src/handlers/equipment_api.rs`, implement `machine_catalog` handler.
2. Query all products from DB with a simplified projection: id, name, category_name (joined), day_rate, weight_kg, specs, available (boolean: is any availability > 0 for next 30 days).
3. Return a JSON object: { "version": "1.0", "generated_at": "ISO8601", "total_products": N, "products": [...] }.
4. Each product in the array: { "id": "uuid", "name": "...", "category": "...", "day_rate_gbp": "50.00", "weight_kg": 150.5, "specifications": {...}, "available_next_30_days": true }.
5. This endpoint should NOT be paginated — return all products in a single response for agent consumption.
6. Consider response size: 533 products × ~500 bytes ≈ 260KB. Gzip compression (already enabled via tower-http) will reduce this significantly.
7. Register route: `GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog` → `machine_catalog`.
subtasks.test_strategy: Integration test: seed 10 products across categories, call GET /api/v1/equipment-api/catalog, verify response contains version, generated_at, total_products=10, and all products with correct simplified schema. Verify available_next_30_days flag is correct for products with and without availability.

subtasks.title: Implement GDPR customer data deletion endpoint
subtasks.description: Implement DELETE /api/v1/gdpr/customer/{id} to delete all customer-related data from the public schema (bookings and any other customer-linked records) per GDPR compliance requirements.
subtasks.details:
1. In `src/handlers/gdpr.rs`, implement `delete_customer_data` handler.
2. Extract customer_id (UUID) from path.
3. Within a database transaction:
   a. SELECT bookings where customer_id matches — note the product_ids and date ranges for availability restoration.
   b. For each booking being deleted: UPDATE availability to decrement the booked count for the corresponding product_id and date range.
   c. DELETE FROM bookings WHERE customer_id = $1.
   d. Log the number of records deleted at INFO level (but not the customer data itself, for GDPR compliance).
4. Return 200 with { "deleted": { "bookings": N }, "customer_id": "..." }.
5. If no records found for the customer_id, still return 200 (idempotent deletion).
6. This endpoint should be admin-protected (apply AdminAuthLayer).
7. Register route: `DELETE /api/v1/gdpr/customer/:id` → `delete_customer_data`.
subtasks.test_strategy: Integration test: create bookings for a customer, call DELETE /api/v1/gdpr/customer/{id}, verify bookings are removed and availability is restored. Call again for the same customer, verify 200 with zero deletions (idempotent). Verify unauthenticated request returns 403.

subtasks.title: Implement health check and Prometheus metrics endpoints
subtasks.description: Implement GET /health/live (liveness), GET /health/ready (readiness checking PostgreSQL and Valkey), and GET /metrics (Prometheus-format metrics including request count, latency histogram, and error rate).
subtasks.details:
1. Create `src/handlers/health.rs`.
2. `liveness` handler: return 200 with { "status": "alive" }. No dependency checks.
3. `readiness` handler: attempt a simple query on PostgreSQL (`SELECT 1`) and a PING on Valkey. If both succeed, return 200 with { "status": "ready", "postgres": "up", "valkey": "up" }. If either fails, return 503 with the failing component marked as "down".
4. Create `src/metrics.rs`: initialize prometheus registry with:
   - `http_requests_total` Counter (labels: method, path, status)
   - `http_request_duration_seconds` Histogram (labels: method, path) with buckets [0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, 5.0]
   - `http_errors_total` Counter (labels: method, path, error_type)
5. Create a Tower middleware that instruments every request: increment counter, observe duration in histogram, increment error counter on 4xx/5xx.
6. `metrics` handler: encode prometheus registry to text format and return with Content-Type: text/plain; version=0.0.4.
7. Register routes: `GET /health/live`, `GET /health/ready`, `GET /metrics`.
8. Ensure /health and /metrics endpoints are NOT rate-limited (exclude from rate limit middleware).
subtasks.test_strategy: Integration test: call GET /health/live, verify 200. Call GET /health/ready with DB up, verify 200 with both components 'up'. Stop Valkey, call /health/ready, verify 503 with valkey 'down'. Call GET /metrics, verify response contains 'http_requests_total' and 'http_request_duration_seconds' in Prometheus text format. Make 5 requests to any endpoint, verify metrics counters increment.

subtasks.title: Create multi-stage Dockerfile for equipment-catalog binary
subtasks.description: Write a multi-stage Dockerfile using rust:1.75-slim for the build stage and debian:bookworm-slim for the runtime stage, producing a minimal container image for the equipment-catalog binary.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `Dockerfile` at the service root.
2. Stage 1 (builder): FROM rust:1.75-slim AS builder. Install build dependencies: `apt-get update && apt-get install -y pkg-config libssl-dev`. WORKDIR /app. Copy Cargo.toml and Cargo.lock first for dependency caching. Run `cargo build --release` with a dummy main.rs to cache deps. Then copy full source and build again.
3. Stage 2 (runtime): FROM debian:bookworm-slim. Install runtime dependencies: `apt-get update && apt-get install -y ca-certificates libssl3 && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*`. Copy binary from builder: `COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/equipment-catalog /usr/local/bin/equipment-catalog`.
4. Create non-root user: `RUN useradd -r -s /bin/false appuser && chown appuser /usr/local/bin/equipment-catalog`. USER appuser.
5. EXPOSE 8080. ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/local/bin/equipment-catalog"].
6. Add `.dockerignore` to exclude target/, .git/, etc.
7. Verify image builds successfully and binary starts inside container.
subtasks.test_strategy: Build the Docker image with `docker build -t equipment-catalog:test .` and verify it succeeds. Run the container with mock env vars and verify the process starts (check logs for 'listening on 0.0.0.0:8080'). Verify the final image size is under 150MB. Verify the process runs as non-root user.

subtasks.title: Create Kubernetes Deployment and Service manifests
subtasks.description: Write Kubernetes manifests for the equipment-catalog Deployment (2 replicas, resource limits, probes, envFrom ConfigMap) and Service in the sigma1 namespace.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `k8s/deployment.yaml`: apiVersion apps/v1, kind Deployment, metadata namespace sigma1, name equipment-catalog.
2. Spec: replicas 2, selector matchLabels app=equipment-catalog.
3. Pod template: container name equipment-catalog, image placeholder (to be set by CI), ports containerPort 8080.
4. envFrom: configMapRef name sigma1-infra-endpoints (provides DATABASE_URL, VALKEY_URL, R2_ENDPOINT, R2_BUCKET, etc.).
5. Additional env vars from Secrets if needed: ALLOWED_SERVICE_IDENTITIES, ALLOWED_USER_IDS.
6. Resources: requests 256Mi memory / 500m CPU, limits 512Mi memory / 1000m CPU.
7. livenessProbe: httpGet path /health/live port 8080, initialDelaySeconds 5, periodSeconds 10.
8. readinessProbe: httpGet path /health/ready port 8080, initialDelaySeconds 10, periodSeconds 5.
9. Create `k8s/service.yaml`: kind Service, metadata namespace sigma1, name equipment-catalog. Spec: type ClusterIP, port 80 targetPort 8080, selector app=equipment-catalog.
10. Add labels: app=equipment-catalog, part-of=sigma1, managed-by=manual.
subtasks.test_strategy: Validate manifests with `kubectl apply --dry-run=client -f k8s/`. Verify all required fields are present. Deploy to a test cluster and verify 2 pods come up ready. Verify the Service routes traffic to both pods. Verify liveness/readiness probes are being called (check pod events).

subtasks.title: Create seed data script with 24 categories and 533+ products
subtasks.description: Create a seed data fixture file (JSON) containing 24 equipment categories and 533+ products with realistic data, and a Rust binary or SQL script to load it into PostgreSQL.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `seed/categories.json` with 24 equipment rental categories: e.g., Excavators, Loaders, Cranes, Scaffolding, Power Tools, Compressors, Generators, Lighting, Surveying Equipment, Concrete Equipment, Dumpers, Telehandlers, Rollers, Access Platforms, Welding Equipment, Pumps, Site Accommodation, Safety Equipment, Fencing, Traffic Management, Lifting Accessories, Small Tools, Cleaning Equipment, Environmental. Include parent_id for subcategories where logical.
2. Create `seed/products.json` with 533+ products distributed across categories. Each product: name, category (by name, to be resolved), description, day_rate (realistic GBP prices), weight_kg, dimensions (object with l/w/h), specs (category-appropriate JSONB, e.g., engine power for excavators, max lift height for cranes).
3. Create `src/bin/seed.rs` or `seed/seed.sql`:
   a. Read categories.json, INSERT into categories table, build name→id mapping.
   b. Read products.json, resolve category names to IDs, INSERT into products table.
   c. For each product, create default availability records for the next 90 days with reasonable quantities (e.g., 1-20 depending on category).
4. Add a cargo binary target `seed` or make it runnable via `cargo run --bin seed -- --database-url $DATABASE_URL`.
5. Make the script idempotent: use ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING or check for existing data.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run the seed script against a clean database, verify 24 categories and 533+ products are inserted. Query products per category and verify reasonable distribution. Verify availability records exist for each product. Run the script again and verify no duplicates are created (idempotency).

title: Build Rental Management System (Grizz - Go/gRPC)
description: Implement the full Rental Management System replacing Current RMS: opportunities (quotes) with lead scoring, projects with check-out/check-in, inventory transactions with barcode scanning, crew scheduling/assignment, delivery tracking with route optimization, and Google Calendar integration. Exposes gRPC services internally and REST via grpc-gateway. Owns the `rms` schema in PostgreSQL.
details:
1. Initialize Go module `sigma1-rms`. Dependencies: `google.golang.org/grpc`, `github.com/grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2`, `github.com/jackc/pgx/v5`, `github.com/redis/go-redis/v9`, `github.com/shopspring/decimal`, `github.com/google/uuid`, `google.golang.org/api/calendar/v3`, `github.com/prometheus/client_golang`.
2. Define protobuf files for 5 gRPC services as specified in PRD:
   - `OpportunityService`: CreateOpportunity, GetOpportunity, UpdateOpportunity, ListOpportunities, ScoreLead.
   - `ProjectService`: CreateProject, GetProject, UpdateProject, CheckOut, CheckIn.
   - `InventoryService`: GetStockLevel, RecordTransaction, ScanBarcode.
   - `CrewService`: ListCrew, AssignCrew, ScheduleCrew.
   - `DeliveryService`: ScheduleDelivery, UpdateDeliveryStatus, OptimizeRoute.
3. Generate gRPC stubs and grpc-gateway REST handlers. Configure gateway annotations for all REST endpoints per PRD:
   - POST/GET/PATCH /api/v1/opportunities, /api/v1/opportunities/:id/approve, /api/v1/opportunities/:id/convert
   - GET /api/v1/projects, /api/v1/projects/:id, POST checkout/checkin
   - GET/POST /api/v1/inventory/transactions
   - GET /api/v1/crew, POST /api/v1/crew/assign
   - POST /api/v1/deliveries/schedule, GET /api/v1/deliveries/:id/route
4. Write PostgreSQL migrations for `rms` schema: `opportunities` (matching PRD Opportunity struct: id, customer_id, status enum, event dates, venue, total_estimate, lead_score, notes), `projects` (matching PRD Project struct), `project_line_items`, `inventory_items`, `inventory_transactions` (matching PRD InventoryTransaction struct), `crew_members`, `crew_assignments`, `schedules`, `deliveries`, `delivery_routes`, `stores`.
5. Implement business logic:
   - Lead scoring algorithm: ScoreLead RPC computes GREEN/YELLOW/RED based on customer data, event size, venue, and signals from Customer Vetting service (calls Finance+Vetting service REST API per D4/D5 — no cross-schema join).
   - Opportunity → Project conversion: validates opportunity is approved, creates project with line items, updates opportunity status to `converted`.
   - Check-out/check-in: creates InventoryTransaction records, updates stock levels, supports barcode scan input.
   - Conflict detection: when creating/updating opportunities or projects, check for overlapping date ranges on the same inventory items.
   - Crew scheduling: assign crew to projects with conflict detection on date ranges.
   - Delivery scheduling with basic route optimization (nearest-neighbor heuristic for v1).
6. Google Calendar integration: on project confirmation, create Google Calendar event with venue, crew, and equipment details. Use service account credentials from External Secrets.
7. Implement GDPR endpoint: `DELETE /api/v1/gdpr/customer/{id}` — delete all opportunities, projects, transactions, and crew assignments related to customer_id.
8. Implement admin allowlist middleware on grpc-gateway: check X-Service-Identity/X-User-ID headers per D11.
9. Health and metrics: `/health/live`, `/health/ready` (check PostgreSQL + Valkey), `/metrics` (Prometheus).
10. Valkey usage: cache stock levels (TTL 30s), session data.
11. Dockerfile: multi-stage build (golang:1.22 for build, gcr.io/distroless/base-debian12 for runtime).
12. Kubernetes Deployment: namespace `sigma1`, 2 replicas, envFrom `sigma1-infra-endpoints`, resource limits.
13. NATS integration: publish audit events to `sigma1.audit.rms.*` on opportunity creation, project conversion, check-out/check-in (fire-and-forget per D3).
test_strategy: 1. Unit test lead scoring: given a customer with verified business + high Google Reviews, score is GREEN; unverified business returns RED. 2. Integration test opportunity lifecycle: Create → update status to qualified → approve → convert to project; verify project exists with correct line items and opportunity status is `converted`. 3. Integration test check-out/check-in: check out 5 items, verify stock decremented; check in 3, verify stock incremented by 3. 4. Conflict detection test: create two opportunities for same items on overlapping dates, verify second returns conflict error. 5. Verify grpc-gateway REST endpoints match PRD URL patterns: POST /api/v1/opportunities returns 201 with valid JSON. 6. Verify Google Calendar event creation: mock Google Calendar API, confirm event payload contains venue and crew. 7. Verify /health/ready returns 503 when PostgreSQL is unreachable. 8. Verify /metrics returns gRPC method call counts and latency histograms. 9. GDPR test: create customer data across opportunities and projects, call DELETE endpoint, verify all data removed. 10. Barcode scan test: POST inventory transaction with barcode, verify correct inventory item resolved.
decision_points: Lead scoring algorithm weights and thresholds: what exact criteria and weightings determine GREEN/YELLOW/RED? Should these be configurable via database/config or hardcoded for v1?
Route optimization strategy: the PRD specifies nearest-neighbor heuristic for v1, but should we account for time windows, vehicle capacity, or multi-stop constraints, or strictly keep it simple?
Google Calendar integration scope: should it be a shared team calendar or per-crew-member individual calendars? How should calendar event updates/cancellations be handled when projects change?
Barcode format: what barcode standard (Code 128, QR, UPC) should ScanBarcode expect, and should the system support multiple formats?
Cross-service communication with Finance+Vetting: what is the exact REST API contract (URL, auth headers, request/response shape) for the customer vetting signals consumed by lead scoring?

subtasks.title: Initialize Go module and protobuf definitions for all 5 gRPC services
subtasks.description: Set up the sigma1-rms Go module with all dependencies and define protobuf files for OpportunityService, ProjectService, InventoryService, CrewService, and DeliveryService with full message types, enums, and RPC method signatures.
subtasks.details:
1. Run `go mod init sigma1-rms` and add all required dependencies: google.golang.org/grpc, grpc-ecosystem/grpc-gateway/v2, jackc/pgx/v5, redis/go-redis/v9, shopspring/decimal, google/uuid, prometheus/client_golang.
2. Create `proto/` directory structure with separate `.proto` files per service:
   - `opportunity.proto`: CreateOpportunity, GetOpportunity, UpdateOpportunity, ListOpportunities, ScoreLead RPCs. Define Opportunity message (id, customer_id, status enum [DRAFT, QUALIFIED, APPROVED, CONVERTED, LOST], event_start, event_end, venue, total_estimate, lead_score, notes). Define LeadScoreResult message with GREEN/YELLOW/RED enum.
   - `project.proto`: CreateProject, GetProject, UpdateProject, CheckOut, CheckIn RPCs. Define Project message (id, opportunity_id, status, line_items, dates). Define CheckOutRequest/CheckInRequest with item_ids and quantities.
   - `inventory.proto`: GetStockLevel, RecordTransaction, ScanBarcode RPCs. Define InventoryTransaction message matching PRD struct.
   - `crew.proto`: ListCrew, AssignCrew, ScheduleCrew RPCs.
   - `delivery.proto`: ScheduleDelivery, UpdateDeliveryStatus, OptimizeRoute RPCs.
3. Add common.proto for shared types (pagination, timestamps, error details).
4. Set up buf.gen.yaml or Makefile for protoc generation with Go and grpc-gateway plugins.
5. Ensure all proto files compile cleanly.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `buf lint` or `protoc` and verify all 5 proto files compile without errors. Verify generated Go stubs exist for all 5 services with correct method signatures. Verify `go build ./...` succeeds with all dependencies resolved.

subtasks.title: Configure grpc-gateway REST annotations and code generation
subtasks.description: Add google.api.http annotations to all protobuf RPC methods mapping to the PRD-specified REST endpoints, generate grpc-gateway reverse proxy handlers, and set up the combined gRPC+REST server entrypoint.
subtasks.details:
1. Add `google/api/annotations.proto` and `google/api/http.proto` imports to each service proto file.
2. Annotate each RPC with the correct HTTP mapping:
   - OpportunityService: POST /api/v1/opportunities, GET /api/v1/opportunities, GET /api/v1/opportunities/{id}, PATCH /api/v1/opportunities/{id}, POST /api/v1/opportunities/{id}/approve, POST /api/v1/opportunities/{id}/convert
   - ProjectService: GET /api/v1/projects, GET /api/v1/projects/{id}, POST /api/v1/projects/{id}/checkout, POST /api/v1/projects/{id}/checkin
   - InventoryService: GET /api/v1/inventory/transactions, POST /api/v1/inventory/transactions, POST /api/v1/inventory/scan
   - CrewService: GET /api/v1/crew, POST /api/v1/crew/assign
   - DeliveryService: POST /api/v1/deliveries/schedule, GET /api/v1/deliveries/{id}/route
3. Regenerate stubs with gateway code.
4. Create `cmd/server/main.go` that starts both a gRPC server (port 50051) and an HTTP gateway server (port 8080) using runtime.NewServeMux(). Register all 5 service handlers.
5. Wire up graceful shutdown, context propagation, and JSON marshaler options (e.g., emit defaults, original proto field names).
subtasks.test_strategy: Start the server and verify each REST endpoint responds (even if with 'not implemented'). Confirm POST /api/v1/opportunities returns a valid JSON response structure. Verify gRPC reflection or grpcurl can list all 5 services on port 50051.

subtasks.title: Write PostgreSQL migrations for rms schema (all tables)
subtasks.description: Create SQL migration files for the complete rms schema covering opportunities, projects, project_line_items, inventory_items, inventory_transactions, crew_members, crew_assignments, schedules, deliveries, delivery_routes, and stores tables with all indexes, constraints, and enums.
subtasks.details:
1. Use golang-migrate or atlas for migration tooling. Create `migrations/` directory.
2. Migration 001: CREATE SCHEMA rms; CREATE TYPE rms.opportunity_status AS ENUM ('draft','qualified','approved','converted','lost'); CREATE TYPE rms.lead_score AS ENUM ('green','yellow','red');
3. Migration 002: CREATE TABLE rms.stores (id UUID PK, name TEXT, address TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ);
4. Migration 003: CREATE TABLE rms.opportunities (id UUID PK DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(), customer_id UUID NOT NULL, status rms.opportunity_status DEFAULT 'draft', event_start TIMESTAMPTZ, event_end TIMESTAMPTZ, venue TEXT, total_estimate NUMERIC(12,2), lead_score rms.lead_score, notes TEXT, store_id UUID REFERENCES rms.stores, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(), updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()). Add indexes on customer_id, status, event_start/event_end for conflict queries.
5. Migration 004: CREATE TABLE rms.projects (id UUID PK, opportunity_id UUID REFERENCES rms.opportunities, status TEXT, event_start TIMESTAMPTZ, event_end TIMESTAMPTZ, venue TEXT, created_at, updated_at). CREATE TABLE rms.project_line_items (id UUID PK, project_id UUID REFERENCES rms.projects, inventory_item_id UUID, quantity INT, unit_price NUMERIC(10,2)).
6. Migration 005: CREATE TABLE rms.inventory_items (id UUID PK, name TEXT, barcode TEXT UNIQUE, category TEXT, total_stock INT, available_stock INT, store_id UUID REFERENCES rms.stores). CREATE TABLE rms.inventory_transactions (id UUID PK, inventory_item_id UUID REFERENCES rms.inventory_items, project_id UUID REFERENCES rms.projects, type TEXT CHECK (type IN ('check_out','check_in','adjustment')), quantity INT, scanned_barcode TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()). Add index on barcode, on inventory_item_id+project_id.
7. Migration 006: CREATE TABLE rms.crew_members (id UUID PK, name TEXT, email TEXT, phone TEXT, skills TEXT[], active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true). CREATE TABLE rms.crew_assignments (id UUID PK, crew_member_id UUID REFERENCES rms.crew_members, project_id UUID REFERENCES rms.projects, role TEXT, assigned_at TIMESTAMPTZ). CREATE TABLE rms.schedules (id UUID PK, crew_member_id UUID, project_id UUID, start_time TIMESTAMPTZ, end_time TIMESTAMPTZ). Add index on crew_member_id+start_time+end_time for conflict detection.
8. Migration 007: CREATE TABLE rms.deliveries (id UUID PK, project_id UUID REFERENCES rms.projects, scheduled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status TEXT, driver TEXT, vehicle TEXT). CREATE TABLE rms.delivery_routes (id UUID PK, delivery_id UUID REFERENCES rms.deliveries, sequence INT, address TEXT, lat DOUBLE PRECISION, lng DOUBLE PRECISION, eta TIMESTAMPTZ).
9. Add GiST or btree indexes on date range columns for efficient overlap queries using tstzrange.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run all migrations against a fresh PostgreSQL instance. Verify all 11 tables exist in the rms schema with correct columns and types. Verify indexes are created. Run migrations up and down to test reversibility. Insert sample rows into each table to validate constraints and foreign keys.

subtasks.title: Implement database repository layer with pgx connection pool
subtasks.description: Create the data access layer with pgx connection pool setup, repository interfaces, and concrete implementations for all rms tables, including parameterized queries and transaction support.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `internal/db/pool.go` with pgxpool.New() accepting DSN from environment (via sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap). Configure pool size, connection timeouts.
2. Define repository interfaces in `internal/repository/` for each domain:
   - OpportunityRepo: Create, GetByID, Update, List (with pagination/filtering), UpdateStatus
   - ProjectRepo: Create, GetByID, Update, ListByOpportunity
   - LineItemRepo: CreateBatch, ListByProject
   - InventoryRepo: GetByID, GetByBarcode, UpdateStock, ListByStore
   - TransactionRepo: Create, ListByProject, ListByItem
   - CrewRepo: List, GetByID, CreateAssignment, ListAssignmentsByProject, GetScheduleConflicts
   - DeliveryRepo: Create, GetByID, UpdateStatus, CreateRoute, GetRouteByDelivery
3. Implement each repository using pgx with prepared statements. Use pgx.Batch for bulk inserts (e.g., line items).
4. Implement a Transaction helper function that wraps pgx.Tx for multi-table atomic operations (e.g., opportunity conversion, check-out with stock update).
5. Use `shopspring/decimal` for all monetary fields. Map UUID fields with `google/uuid`.
6. Add date-range overlap query helpers using `(start1, end1) OVERLAPS (start2, end2)` or tstzrange for conflict detection queries.
subtasks.test_strategy: Write integration tests against a test PostgreSQL instance. Test CRUD operations for each repository. Verify transaction rollback on error. Verify date-range overlap query returns correct conflicts. Verify bulk line item insert creates all rows atomically.

subtasks.title: Implement OpportunityService: CRUD, lead scoring, and approval workflow
subtasks.description: Implement the OpportunityService gRPC server with full CRUD operations, lead scoring algorithm (GREEN/YELLOW/RED), status transitions (draft→qualified→approved→converted/lost), and input validation.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `internal/service/opportunity.go` implementing the generated OpportunityServiceServer interface.
2. CreateOpportunity: validate required fields (customer_id, event_start, event_end, venue), set status to DRAFT, persist via OpportunityRepo, return created opportunity.
3. GetOpportunity: lookup by ID, return NOT_FOUND if missing.
4. UpdateOpportunity: field-mask based update, validate status transitions (only allowed: draft→qualified, qualified→approved, qualified→lost, approved→converted).
5. ListOpportunities: support pagination (page_size, page_token), filtering by status, customer_id, date range.
6. ScoreLead RPC implementation:
   - Accept opportunity_id, fetch opportunity data.
   - Call Finance+Vetting service REST API (HTTP client) to get customer vetting signals (business verified, Google review count, payment history). Use environment variable for vetting service URL.
   - Scoring logic: GREEN if business_verified AND google_reviews >= 4.0 AND no payment_issues; RED if !business_verified OR payment_issues; YELLOW otherwise.
   - Update opportunity.lead_score in database.
   - Return LeadScoreResult with score and reasoning.
7. Approve RPC: validate opportunity is in QUALIFIED status, set to APPROVED.
8. Wire service into gRPC server registration in main.go.
9. Add request validation using protovalidate or manual checks with proper gRPC status codes (InvalidArgument, NotFound, FailedPrecondition).
subtasks.test_strategy: Unit test lead scoring: mock vetting service HTTP responses — verified business + high reviews → GREEN, unverified → RED, mixed → YELLOW. Integration test full lifecycle: Create (verify DRAFT) → Update to QUALIFIED → ScoreLead → Approve → verify status is APPROVED. Test invalid transitions return FailedPrecondition. Test ListOpportunities pagination returns correct pages.

subtasks.title: Implement ProjectService: CRUD, opportunity conversion, and conflict detection
subtasks.description: Implement the ProjectService gRPC server with project CRUD, opportunity-to-project conversion logic (atomic transaction creating project + line items + updating opportunity status), and inventory date-range conflict detection.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `internal/service/project.go` implementing ProjectServiceServer.
2. CreateProject: standard project creation with validation.
3. GetProject: lookup by ID with line items eagerly loaded.
4. UpdateProject: field-mask update with date validation.
5. Convert RPC (called from OpportunityService or standalone):
   - Validate opportunity exists and status is APPROVED.
   - Within a database transaction:
     a. Create Project record from opportunity data (dates, venue, customer_id).
     b. Create ProjectLineItems from opportunity details (inventory items, quantities, prices).
     c. Update opportunity status to CONVERTED with project_id reference.
   - If any step fails, rollback entire transaction.
6. Conflict detection helper: before creating/updating a project, query for existing projects that have overlapping date ranges AND share any of the same inventory_item_ids in their line items. Return a ConflictError with details of conflicting projects and items.
7. Integrate conflict detection into CreateProject and UpdateProject — return FailedPrecondition with conflict details if detected.
8. Wire into gRPC server registration.
subtasks.test_strategy: Integration test opportunity→project conversion: create approved opportunity, convert, verify project exists with correct line items and opportunity status is CONVERTED. Test conversion of non-APPROVED opportunity returns FailedPrecondition.
