subtasks.test_strategy: GET `/llms.txt` — returns 200 with `Content-Type: text/plain`, body contains company name, services, categories, contact info. GET `/llms-full` — returns 200 with text/plain, body contains equipment product entries with names and prices. Both responses are under 1MB. Content is well-structured and human-readable plain text.

subtasks.title: Implement WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility across all pages and components
subtasks.description: Audit and ensure WCAG 2.1 AA compliance across the entire site: proper heading hierarchy, alt text on all images, keyboard navigation, focus management in the chat widget and lightbox, ARIA labels, color contrast ratios, and screen reader compatibility.
subtasks.details:
1. Heading hierarchy audit: ensure every page has exactly one `<h1>`, and headings follow sequential order (h1 > h2 > h3). Fix any skipped levels.
2. Image alt text: verify every `<Image>` component has meaningful alt text. Equipment images: alt = product name. Portfolio images: alt = event description. Decorative images: alt = ''.
3. Keyboard navigation:
   - All interactive elements (buttons, links, inputs) reachable via Tab.
   - Chat widget: Tab into chat input, Enter to send, Escape to close panel.
   - Lightbox: arrow keys navigate, Escape closes, focus trapped inside when open.
   - Quote builder: Tab navigates between form fields, Enter submits step.
   - Image carousel: arrow keys navigate slides.
4. Focus management:
   - Chat widget: focus moves to input when panel opens, returns to trigger button on close.
   - Lightbox: focus moves to lightbox on open, returns to triggering card on close.
   - Multi-step form: focus moves to first field of new step on navigation.
5. ARIA labels: add `aria-label` to icon-only buttons (chat toggle, carousel arrows, close buttons). Add `aria-live='polite'` to chat message area for screen reader announcements of new messages. Add `role='dialog'` to chat panel and lightbox.
6. Color contrast: verify all text meets 4.5:1 contrast ratio against backgrounds (use TailwindCSS color tokens). Availability calendar: don't rely solely on red/green — add icons or patterns for colorblind users.
7. Skip navigation link: add 'Skip to main content' link at top of page.
8. Form labels: all inputs in quote builder have associated `<label>` elements. Error messages linked via `aria-describedby`.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run axe-core accessibility audit on every page — zero critical or serious violations. Keyboard-only navigation test: Tab through entire home page, equipment page, and quote builder without getting stuck. Screen reader test (VoiceOver/NVDA): chat widget announces new messages, lightbox navigation described. Color contrast checker: all text passes 4.5:1. Lighthouse Accessibility score >= 95 on home page and equipment page. Focus indicator visible on all interactive elements.

subtasks.title: Configure Cloudflare Pages deployment and performance optimization
subtasks.description: Set up Cloudflare Pages deployment with the appropriate adapter, configure environment variables, optimize performance with image handling, ISR, static generation, and dynamic imports. Achieve Lighthouse performance score >= 90.
subtasks.details:
1. Install and configure Cloudflare Pages adapter: `bun add @cloudflare/next-on-pages` (or chosen adapter per decision point). Configure `next.config.js` accordingly.
2. Create `wrangler.toml` or Cloudflare Pages project configuration: set build command (`bun run build`), output directory, compatibility date, compatibility flags for Node.js APIs.
3. Environment variables: configure in Cloudflare Pages dashboard (production + preview): NEXT_PUBLIC_API_BASE_URL, NEXT_PUBLIC_CHAT_API_URL, NEXT_PUBLIC_SOCIAL_API_URL, NEXT_PUBLIC_R2_CDN_URL.
4. Image optimization:
   - Configure `next.config.js` `images.remotePatterns` for R2 CDN domain.
   - Verify all pages use Next.js `<Image>` with explicit width/height or fill.
   - Set appropriate `sizes` attributes for responsive images.
5. Static generation verification: confirm `/`, `/llms.txt`, `/llms-full` are statically generated at build time.
6. ISR verification: `/equipment` pages revalidate at 60s, `/portfolio` at 300s, home at 3600s.
7. Dynamic import for chat widget: verify ChatWidget is code-split and not in initial bundle (check build output).
8. Bundle analysis: run `@next/bundle-analyzer` to identify large dependencies. Verify effect and @effect/schema are tree-shaken appropriately.
9. Create deployment script or CI configuration for Cloudflare Pages.
10. Test production build locally: `bun run build && bun run start` — verify all routes work with production optimizations.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `bun run build` — succeeds with output compatible with Cloudflare Pages. Static routes (/, /llms.txt, /llms-full) present in static output. Chat widget chunk is separate from main bundle (verify in build output). Deploy to Cloudflare Pages preview — all pages load correctly, images serve from R2 CDN, API calls route through Cloudflare Tunnel URLs. Lighthouse audit on deployed preview: Performance >= 90, FCP < 1.5s, LCP < 2.5s. ISR verified: update product in API, wait 60s, refresh equipment page — new data appears.

title: Build CI/CD Pipeline and QA Automation (Atlas - CI/CD)
description: Implement the complete CI/CD pipeline for all Sigma-1 services: GitHub Actions workflows for build/test/lint/security-scan per service, ArgoCD GitOps deployment, automated code review triggers, merge gate policy enforcement, and the GDPR orchestrator cron job. Integrates the QA agents (Stitch, Cleo, Tess, Cipher) into the pipeline.
details:
1. GitHub Actions workflow per service (5 workflows):
   a. `equipment-catalog.yml`: Rust CI — cargo fmt check, cargo clippy (deny warnings), cargo test, cargo build --release, Docker build+push.
   b. `rms.yml`: Go CI — go fmt, go vet, staticcheck, go test -race -coverprofile, protobuf lint (buf lint), Docker build+push.
   c. `finance-vetting.yml`: Rust CI (same pattern as equipment-catalog).
   d. `social-engine.yml`: Node.js CI — biome check (lint+format), bun test, Docker build+push.
   e. `website.yml`: Next.js CI — biome check, bun test, next build, Cloudflare Pages deploy.
   Each workflow runs on: push to main, pull_request to main.
2. Shared CI steps (reusable workflows):
   - **Stitch code review**: On PR, trigger automated review comment via GitHub App integration.
   - **Cleo quality check**: Run language-specific linters (clippy, go vet, biome) with quality thresholds.
   - **Tess test enforcement**: Enforce minimum 80% code coverage. Fail CI if coverage drops below threshold. Use cargo-llvm-cov for Rust, go test -cover for Go, c8/vitest coverage for Node.js.
   - **Cipher security scan**: Run Semgrep with OWASP rules, CodeQL analysis, dependency audit (cargo-audit, govulncheck, npm audit). Block merge on critical/high severity.
3. Merge gate policy (GitHub branch protection rules):
   - Require: all CI checks passing, at least 1 approval (automated or human), no critical security findings.
   - Automatic merge conflict detection.
4. ArgoCD GitOps setup:
   - ArgoCD Application CRs for each service in `sigma1` namespace.
   - Deployment manifests in Git repo under `deploy/` directory.
   - Automatic sync on main branch push.
   - Automatic rollback on health check failure (progressDeadlineSeconds: 300).
   - Separate Application for Morgan in `openclaw` namespace.
5. GDPR orchestrator (per D15):
   - Kubernetes CronJob `gdpr-orchestrator` in `sigma1` namespace.
   - Simple shell script or lightweight Go binary that:
     a. Reads customer IDs from a `gdpr_deletion_requests` table in `audit` schema.
     b. Sequentially calls DELETE /api/v1/gdpr/customer/{id} on each service: equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, social-engine.
     c. Checks HTTP 200 response from each.
     d. Logs success/failure to `audit.gdpr_deletion_log` table.
     e. Marks request as completed or failed.
   - Schedule: daily at 02:00 UTC (GDPR deletions are rare, daily check sufficient).
6. Container registry: configure GitHub Container Registry (ghcr.io) for all service images.
7. Environment promotion: dev → staging → prod via ArgoCD ApplicationSets or manual promotion.
test_strategy: 1. Verify each CI workflow runs successfully on a test PR: push a trivial change to each service, confirm all checks (lint, test, build, security) pass. 2. Verify merge gate blocks PR with failing tests: push a PR with a deliberately failing test, confirm merge is blocked. 3. Verify Cipher security scan blocks on critical finding: introduce a known vulnerable dependency, confirm CI fails with security finding. 4. Verify coverage enforcement: push a PR that drops coverage below 80%, confirm CI fails. 5. Verify ArgoCD sync: push a deployment manifest change to main, confirm ArgoCD detects and applies within 3 minutes. 6. Verify ArgoCD rollback: deploy a broken image (incorrect health check), confirm rollback triggers after progressDeadlineSeconds. 7. GDPR orchestrator test: insert a test deletion request, run the cron job manually, verify all 4 services' GDPR endpoints called and audit log entry created. 8. Verify container images are pushed to ghcr.io with correct tags (commit SHA + latest). 9. Verify Stitch review comment appears on test PR within 2 minutes of PR creation. 10. Verify all workflows use reusable workflow pattern (DRY, no duplicated CI steps).

decision_points.question: How should the Stitch automated code review agent be triggered — as a GitHub App receiving PR webhook events, as a GitHub Action step calling an external API, or as a self-hosted bot polling for PRs? This affects auth setup, latency, and infrastructure requirements.
options: GitHub Action step calling Stitch API
decision_points.impact: Determines the architecture for automated code review comments and whether additional infrastructure (webhook receiver) is needed.

decision_points.question: Should the GDPR orchestrator be a lightweight shell script (curl-based) or a compiled Go binary? Shell is simpler but harder to test and lacks structured error handling; Go is more robust but adds a build step.
decision_points.impact: Affects testability, error handling quality, and whether the GDPR orchestrator needs its own CI workflow and container image.

decision_points.question: Should environment promotion (dev → staging → prod) use ArgoCD ApplicationSets with Git directory-based generators, separate ArgoCD Applications with manual sync, or a PR-based promotion flow (e.g., promote by merging to environment branches)?
options: Separate ArgoCD Applications with manual promotion
decision_points.impact: Defines the deployment topology, how many ArgoCD resources to manage, and the operational workflow for production releases.

subtasks.title: Create reusable GitHub Actions workflow for Rust CI (fmt, clippy, test, build, Docker push)
subtasks.description: Build a reusable/composite GitHub Actions workflow for Rust services that runs cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy -- -D warnings, cargo test, cargo build --release, and Docker build+push to ghcr.io. This will be consumed by equipment-catalog and finance-vetting workflows.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `.github/workflows/rust-ci.yml` as a reusable workflow (workflow_call) accepting inputs: service-name, working-directory, Dockerfile path.
2. Steps: checkout → setup Rust toolchain (stable, with clippy/rustfmt components) → cargo fmt --check → cargo clippy -- -D warnings → cargo test → cargo build --release → Docker login to ghcr.io using GITHUB_TOKEN → Docker build with build args for the release binary → Docker push with tags: commit SHA and 'latest'.
3. Use actions/cache for cargo registry and target directory to speed up builds.
4. Ensure the workflow outputs: image-tag, test-result, coverage-report-path (for Tess integration).
5. Configure to run on `workflow_call` trigger so service-specific workflows can invoke it.
subtasks.test_strategy: Invoke the reusable workflow manually with a test Rust project. Verify all steps execute: fmt check passes on well-formatted code, clippy passes with no warnings, tests run, Docker image is built and pushed to ghcr.io with correct SHA tag.

subtasks.title: Create reusable GitHub Actions workflow for Go CI (fmt, vet, staticcheck, test, buf lint, Docker push)
subtasks.description: Build a reusable/composite GitHub Actions workflow for Go services that runs go fmt, go vet, staticcheck, go test -race -coverprofile, buf lint for protobuf, and Docker build+push to ghcr.io. Consumed by the rms workflow.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `.github/workflows/go-ci.yml` as a reusable workflow accepting inputs: service-name, working-directory, Dockerfile path.
2. Steps: checkout → setup Go (latest stable) → go fmt check (gofmt -l, fail if output) → go vet ./... → install and run staticcheck → go test -race -coverprofile=coverage.out ./... → install buf CLI and run buf lint on proto/ directory → Docker login to ghcr.io → Docker build+push with SHA and latest tags.
3. Use actions/cache for Go module cache.
4. Output coverage.out path for Tess coverage enforcement.
5. Include buf breaking change detection against main branch (buf breaking --against '.git#branch=main').
subtasks.test_strategy: Invoke the reusable workflow with the rms Go module. Verify go vet passes, staticcheck runs, tests execute with race detector, buf lint validates proto files, and Docker image is pushed to ghcr.io.

subtasks.title: Create reusable GitHub Actions workflow for Node.js/Bun CI (biome, test, Docker push)
subtasks.description: Build a reusable/composite GitHub Actions workflow for Node.js/Bun services that runs biome check, bun test, and Docker build+push to ghcr.io. Consumed by social-engine workflow.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `.github/workflows/node-ci.yml` as a reusable workflow accepting inputs: service-name, working-directory, Dockerfile path.
2. Steps: checkout → setup Bun (latest stable) → bun install → biome check (lint + format enforcement) → bun test (with coverage via c8/vitest if configured) → Docker login to ghcr.io → Docker build+push with SHA and latest tags.
3. Use actions/cache for node_modules and bun cache.
4. Output coverage report path for Tess integration.
subtasks.test_strategy: Invoke the reusable workflow with the social-engine project. Verify biome lint/format passes, bun test runs successfully, and Docker image is pushed to ghcr.io with correct tags.

subtasks.title: Create service-specific CI workflows invoking reusable workflows (equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, social-engine)
subtasks.description: Create the 4 service-specific GitHub Actions workflow files that invoke the appropriate reusable workflows with correct parameters. Each triggers on push to main and pull_request to main with path filters.
subtasks.details:
1. `.github/workflows/equipment-catalog.yml`: triggers on paths `services/equipment-catalog/**`, calls rust-ci.yml with service-name=equipment-catalog, working-directory=services/equipment-catalog.
2. `.github/workflows/rms.yml`: triggers on paths `services/rms/**`, calls go-ci.yml with service-name=rms, working-directory=services/rms.
3. `.github/workflows/finance-vetting.yml`: triggers on paths `services/finance-vetting/**`, calls rust-ci.yml with service-name=finance-vetting, working-directory=services/finance-vetting.
4. `.github/workflows/social-engine.yml`: triggers on paths `services/social-engine/**`, calls node-ci.yml with service-name=social-engine, working-directory=services/social-engine.
5. Each workflow should pass through secrets (GITHUB_TOKEN for ghcr.io) using `secrets: inherit`.
6. Add concurrency groups per service per PR to cancel stale runs.
subtasks.test_strategy: Push a trivial change to each service directory on a test branch, open PRs, and verify each workflow triggers only for its service path and invokes the correct reusable workflow. Confirm concurrency cancels stale runs.

subtasks.title: Create Next.js CI workflow for website (biome, test, build, Cloudflare Pages deploy)
subtasks.description: Build the website-specific GitHub Actions workflow that runs biome check, bun test, next build, and deploys to Cloudflare Pages. This workflow differs from the Node.js reusable workflow because it deploys to Cloudflare Pages instead of Docker/ghcr.io.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `.github/workflows/website.yml` triggering on push to main and pull_request to main, with path filter `apps/website/**`.
2. Steps: checkout → setup Bun → setup Node.js (for next build compatibility) → bun install → biome check → bun test → next build (output: export or standalone as configured) → deploy to Cloudflare Pages using wrangler-action or Cloudflare Pages GitHub integration.
3. For PRs: deploy preview to Cloudflare Pages preview URL and comment the URL on the PR.
4. For main pushes: deploy to production Cloudflare Pages.
5. Use CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN and CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID secrets.
6. Add concurrency group to prevent duplicate deploys.
subtasks.test_strategy: Push a trivial change to apps/website/ on a test branch, open a PR, verify biome/test/build pass and a Cloudflare Pages preview URL is posted as a PR comment. Merge and verify production deployment triggers.

subtasks.title: Configure GitHub Container Registry (ghcr.io) for all service images
subtasks.description: Set up ghcr.io as the container registry for all backend services, including repository-level permissions, image naming conventions, and retention policies.
subtasks.details:
1. Ensure the GitHub repository/organization has ghcr.io enabled.
2. Define image naming convention: ghcr.io/<org>/sigma1-<service-name>:sha-<commit> and ghcr.io/<org>/sigma1-<service-name>:latest.
3. Configure GITHUB_TOKEN permissions in workflow files: `packages: write` for push, `contents: read`.
4. Create a shared Docker login step in each reusable workflow: `docker login ghcr.io -u ${{ github.actor }} -p ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}`.
5. Set package visibility to match repository visibility.
6. Optionally configure image retention policy (e.g., keep last 20 tagged images, delete untagged after 7 days) using GitHub's container retention settings or a cleanup workflow.
subtasks.test_strategy: Manually trigger a CI workflow, verify the Docker image is pushed to ghcr.io with correct naming convention (sha tag + latest). Verify the image is pullable from ghcr.io with correct authentication.

subtasks.title: Implement Tess coverage enforcement (80% minimum threshold) across all language-specific workflows
subtasks.description: Add coverage collection and threshold enforcement to each reusable CI workflow: cargo-llvm-cov for Rust, go test -coverprofile for Go, and c8/vitest coverage for Node.js. Fail CI if coverage drops below 80%.
subtasks.details:
1. Rust (rust-ci.yml): Install cargo-llvm-cov → run `cargo llvm-cov --lcov --output-path lcov.info` → parse coverage percentage → fail step if < 80%. Upload lcov.info as artifact.
2. Go (go-ci.yml): Parse `go test -coverprofile=coverage.out` output → extract total coverage percentage from `go tool cover -func=coverage.out | grep total` → fail step if < 80%. Upload coverage.out as artifact.
3. Node.js (node-ci.yml): Configure vitest/c8 to output coverage with `--coverage` flag → parse coverage-summary.json for line coverage → fail step if < 80%. Upload coverage report as artifact.
4. For all: Add a coverage badge or PR comment showing current coverage percentage using an action like `marocchino/sticky-pull-request-comment`.
5. Create a shared coverage-check composite action that takes coverage percentage as input and fails with clear message if below threshold.
subtasks.test_strategy: Push a PR that includes code with known <80% coverage for one service, verify CI fails with clear coverage threshold message. Push a PR with >80% coverage, verify CI passes. Verify coverage percentage appears in PR comment.

subtasks.title: Implement Cipher security scanning (Semgrep, CodeQL, dependency audits) as reusable workflow
subtasks.description: Create a reusable security scanning workflow that runs Semgrep with OWASP rules, CodeQL analysis, and language-specific dependency audits (cargo-audit, govulncheck, npm audit). Block merge on critical/high severity findings.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `.github/workflows/security-scan.yml` as a reusable workflow accepting inputs: language (rust|go|node), working-directory.
2. Semgrep step: Run `semgrep ci` with `--config p/owasp-top-ten --config p/security-audit`. Use SEMGREP_APP_TOKEN if available, otherwise run locally. Fail on findings severity >= ERROR.
3. CodeQL step: Use `github/codeql-action/init` and `github/codeql-action/analyze` with auto-detected language. Upload SARIF results.
4. Dependency audit step (conditional on language input):
   - Rust: `cargo install cargo-audit && cargo audit --deny warnings`
   - Go: `go install golang.org/x/vuln/cmd/govulncheck@latest && govulncheck ./...`
   - Node: `bun audit` or `npm audit --audit-level=high`
5. Aggregate results: fail the workflow if any scanner reports critical or high severity findings.
6. Add this as a required status check in the service-specific workflows (call security-scan.yml after build).
subtasks.test_strategy: Introduce a known vulnerable dependency (e.g., an old crate with a CVE) in a test PR. Verify Cipher security scan detects it and fails CI. Remove the vulnerability, verify scan passes. Verify Semgrep catches a test OWASP pattern (e.g., SQL injection in a test file).

subtasks.title: Implement Stitch automated code review trigger on PR creation
subtasks.description: Configure the Stitch automated code review agent to trigger on pull request events and post review comments. Set up the GitHub App integration or Actions-based trigger mechanism.
subtasks.details:
1. Define the Stitch integration approach (dependent on decision point resolution — GitHub App or Action step).
2. If GitHub Action approach: Create a workflow `.github/workflows/stitch-review.yml` triggered on `pull_request` events (opened, synchronize). The workflow sends the PR diff to the Stitch review service endpoint and posts the response as a PR review comment using `actions/github-script` or `peter-evans/create-or-update-comment`.
3. Configure the Stitch service URL as a repository secret `STITCH_REVIEW_URL`.
4. The review request payload should include: PR diff, file list, commit messages, and service context (which service is being changed, detected from path filters).
5. Post review as a PR comment with structured feedback (issues found, suggestions, approval recommendation).
6. Set a timeout of 120 seconds for the Stitch API call; if timeout, post a comment indicating review is pending.
7. This is informational only — Stitch review does NOT block merge (advisory).
subtasks.test_strategy: Open a test PR with a trivial code change. Verify the Stitch review workflow triggers within 2 minutes, and a review comment appears on the PR with structured feedback. Verify timeout handling by testing with an unreachable Stitch URL.

subtasks.title: Configure merge gate and GitHub branch protection rules
subtasks.description: Set up GitHub branch protection rules on the main branch requiring all CI checks to pass, at least 1 approval, no critical security findings, and automatic merge conflict detection.
subtasks.details:
1. Configure branch protection on `main` branch via GitHub API or repository settings:
   - Require status checks to pass before merging: list all CI workflow check names (equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, social-engine, website, security-scan).
   - Require branches to be up to date before merging.
   - Require at least 1 approving review (can be from Stitch bot or human).
   - Require conversation resolution before merging.
   - Do not allow bypassing the above settings (even for admins in production).
2. Create a `.github/branch-protection.json` or document the settings in the repo README for reproducibility.
3. Optionally create a GitHub Actions workflow that uses the GitHub API to programmatically enforce these rules (infrastructure-as-code approach).
4. Enable automatic merge conflict detection (this is default GitHub behavior, but ensure linear history or merge commits policy is defined).
5. Configure CODEOWNERS file mapping service directories to respective team/agent owners.
subtasks.test_strategy: Push a PR with a deliberately failing test — verify merge button is disabled and status checks show failure. Push a PR with all checks passing but no approval — verify merge is blocked until approval is given. Verify CODEOWNERS triggers correct review requests.

subtasks.title: Create ArgoCD Application CRs and deployment manifests for all services
subtasks.description: Set up the ArgoCD GitOps deployment: create the deploy/ directory structure with Kubernetes deployment manifests and ArgoCD Application custom resources for each service in the sigma1 namespace, plus Morgan in the openclaw namespace.
subtasks.details:
1. Create directory structure: `deploy/<service-name>/` for each of: equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, social-engine, website (if k8s-deployed), morgan.
2. Each service directory contains: deployment.yaml, service.yaml, configmap.yaml (referencing sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap via envFrom), hpa.yaml (optional for v1).
3. Deployment manifests reference ghcr.io images with tag placeholder (kustomize or Helm values).
4. Create ArgoCD Application CRs in `deploy/argocd/`:
   - One Application per service, targeting `sigma1` namespace.
   - Source: Git repo, path: `deploy/<service-name>/`, targetRevision: main.
   - Sync policy: automated with selfHeal=true, prune=true.
   - Health check: standard Kubernetes deployment health.
   - progressDeadlineSeconds: 300 in deployment specs for rollback trigger.
5. Create separate ArgoCD Application for Morgan targeting `openclaw` namespace.
6. Use Kustomize overlays or Helm for environment-specific values (dev/staging/prod image tags).
7. Configure ArgoCD to use the ghcr.io credentials for pulling images (imagePullSecrets).
subtasks.test_strategy: Apply ArgoCD Application CRs to the cluster. Verify ArgoCD UI shows all applications as 'Synced' and 'Healthy'. Push a manifest change to deploy/ on main, verify ArgoCD detects the change and syncs within 3 minutes. Verify services are running with correct image tags.

subtasks.title: Configure ArgoCD automatic rollback on health check failure
subtasks.description: Configure ArgoCD sync policies and Kubernetes deployment settings to automatically rollback when a deployment fails health checks within the progress deadline.
subtasks.details:
1. In each Kubernetes Deployment manifest, set `spec.progressDeadlineSeconds: 300` to define the window for a successful rollout.
2. Configure readiness probes and liveness probes on each service container (health check endpoints: /health or /healthz).
3. In ArgoCD Application sync policy, configure:
   - `automated.selfHeal: true` — ArgoCD will revert manual changes.
   - `retry.limit: 3` with backoff for transient failures.
4. ArgoCD detects a 'Degraded' health status when progressDeadlineSeconds is exceeded and the deployment hasn't become ready.
5. Test rollback behavior: ArgoCD with selfHeal will re-sync to the Git state. If the Git state itself is broken, ArgoCD marks the app as 'Degraded' — configure a notification (Slack/email) for degraded apps.
6. Optionally configure ArgoCD Rollouts for blue-green or canary (v2 concern — for v1, standard rolling update with rollback is sufficient).
7. Set up ArgoCD notifications for sync failures using argocd-notifications controller.
subtasks.test_strategy: Deploy a service with a broken Docker image (e.g., wrong entrypoint that fails health checks). Verify the Kubernetes deployment does not complete rollout within 300 seconds. Verify ArgoCD marks the application as 'Degraded'. Redeploy the correct image and verify ArgoCD auto-syncs to healthy state.

subtasks.title: Build GDPR orchestrator CronJob (binary, deletion logic, audit logging, Kubernetes manifest)
subtasks.description: Implement the GDPR orchestrator as a Kubernetes CronJob that reads deletion requests from the audit schema, calls DELETE endpoints on all 4 services, and logs results to the audit table. Runs daily at 02:00 UTC.
subtasks.details:
1. Create a lightweight Go binary in `services/gdpr-orchestrator/` (or shell script based on decision point).
2. The binary:
   a. Connects to PostgreSQL (using DATABASE_URL from sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap).
   b. Queries `audit.gdpr_deletion_requests` for rows with status='pending'.
   c. For each request, sequentially calls:
      - DELETE http://equipment-catalog:8080/api/v1/gdpr/customer/{id}
      - DELETE http://rms:8080/api/v1/gdpr/customer/{id}
      - DELETE http://finance-vetting:8080/api/v1/gdpr/customer/{id}
      - DELETE http://social-engine:3000/api/v1/gdpr/customer/{id}
   d. Checks HTTP 200 from each. If any fails, marks the request as 'partial_failure' with details of which service failed.
   e. On all success, marks request as 'completed' and inserts a row into `audit.gdpr_deletion_log` with timestamp, customer_id, and per-service results.
   f. On failure, retries up to 3 times with exponential backoff per service call.
3. Create Kubernetes CronJob manifest in `deploy/gdpr-orchestrator/cronjob.yaml`:
   - schedule: '0 2 * * *' (daily 02:00 UTC)
   - namespace: sigma1
   - envFrom: sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap
   - restartPolicy: OnFailure
   - activeDeadlineSeconds: 3600
   - successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3, failedJobsHistoryLimit: 5
4. Add Dockerfile for the Go binary and CI workflow (reuse go-ci.yml).
5. Create the `audit.gdpr_deletion_requests` and `audit.gdpr_deletion_log` table migrations (or reference existing migration task).
subtasks.test_strategy: Insert a test GDPR deletion request in the audit.gdpr_deletion_requests table. Manually trigger the CronJob (`kubectl create job --from=cronjob/gdpr-orchestrator test-run`). Verify all 4 service GDPR endpoints are called (check service logs). Verify the audit.gdpr_deletion_log entry is created with correct details. Test partial failure by making one service unavailable — verify request is marked as 'partial_failure'.

title: Production Hardening: HA, CDN, TLS, Ingress, Network Policies (Bolt - Kubernetes/Helm)
description: Scale all infrastructure to production-grade HA: PostgreSQL multi-replica with automatic failover, Valkey with sentinel/replication, configure Cloudflare Tunnel ingress routes for all public endpoints, Cloudflare CDN for static assets and R2, TLS termination, Cilium network policies hardened to service-level granularity, resource limits tuned for production, HPA for backend services, and PodDisruptionBudgets for 99.9% uptime.
details:
1. PostgreSQL HA: Update CloudNative-PG Cluster CR `sigma1-postgres` to `instances: 3` with automatic failover. Configure connection pooling via PgBouncer sidecar (built into CNPG). Set backup schedule to R2 (WAL archiving + daily base backup).
2. Valkey HA: Update OpsTree Redis CR to sentinel mode with 3 replicas. Configure maxmemory policy (allkeys-lru), persistence (AOF), and memory limits.
3. Cloudflare Tunnel ingress routes (per D9):
   - `/api/v1/catalog/*` → equipment-catalog.sigma1:8080
   - `/api/v1/opportunities/*`, `/api/v1/projects/*`, `/api/v1/inventory/*`, `/api/v1/crew/*`, `/api/v1/deliveries/*` → rms.sigma1:8080
   - `/api/v1/invoices/*`, `/api/v1/payments/*`, `/api/v1/finance/*`, `/api/v1/payroll/*`, `/api/v1/currency/*`, `/api/v1/vetting/*` → finance-vetting.sigma1:8080
   - `/api/v1/social/*` → social-engine.sigma1:3000
   - `/api/v1/chat/*` → morgan.openclaw:8080
   - `/api/v1/stripe/webhook` → finance-vetting.sigma1:8080 (Stripe webhooks)
   - Website served from Cloudflare Pages (separate deployment, not tunneled).
4. Cloudflare CDN configuration:
   - R2 custom domain for media CDN (e.g., media.sigma1.com → sigma1-media R2 bucket).
   - Cache rules: product images (1 year), social media photos (1 month), API responses (no cache or short TTL via Cache-Control headers).
   - Page rules: security headers (HSTS, X-Frame-Options, CSP).
5. Cilium network policies — tighten from namespace-level to service-level:
   - equipment-catalog can reach: PostgreSQL, Valkey, R2. Cannot reach: other services.
   - rms can reach: PostgreSQL, Valkey, finance-vetting (for vetting data), Google Calendar API (egress). Cannot reach: social-engine.
   - finance-vetting can reach: PostgreSQL, Valkey, Stripe API, OpenCorporates/LinkedIn/Google/Credit APIs (egress), RMS (for project data). Cannot reach: social-engine directly.
   - social-engine can reach: PostgreSQL, Valkey, R2, Instagram/LinkedIn/Facebook/TikTok/OpenAI APIs (egress).
   - morgan can reach: all sigma1 services, Signal servers (egress), ElevenLabs (egress), Twilio (egress).
   - Default deny all other traffic.
6. Resource limits tuned for production:
   - equipment-catalog: 256Mi-1Gi, 500m-2000m, 2 replicas.
   - rms: 256Mi-1Gi, 500m-2000m, 2 replicas.
   - finance-vetting: 256Mi-1Gi, 500m-2000m, 2 replicas.
   - social-engine: 256Mi-512Mi, 250m-1000m, 1-2 replicas.
   - morgan: 512Mi-2Gi, 1000m-2000m, 1 replica (stateful with Signal-CLI).
7. HorizontalPodAutoscaler for stateless services (equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, social-engine): min 2, max 5, target CPU 70%.
8. PodDisruptionBudget: minAvailable 1 for all services with 2+ replicas.
9. PostgreSQL monitoring: configure Prometheus scraping of CNPG metrics, alert rules for replication lag > 10s, connection pool exhaustion.
10. Grafana dashboards: import/create dashboards for each service (request rate, error rate, latency p50/p95/p99), PostgreSQL metrics, Valkey metrics.
11. Loki log aggregation: ensure all service pods have correct logging labels for Loki collection.
test_strategy: 1. PostgreSQL HA test: kill the primary pod, verify automatic failover completes within 30 seconds and all services reconnect. 2. Valkey HA test: kill sentinel master, verify failover and services reconnect. 3. Cloudflare Tunnel test: from external network, curl each API route, verify 200 responses with correct service. 4. TLS test: verify all external endpoints serve valid TLS certificates (curl --verbose shows TLS 1.3). 5. Cilium policy test: exec into equipment-catalog pod, verify cannot reach social-engine pod; exec into morgan pod, verify can reach all services. 6. HPA test: generate load on equipment-catalog (100 concurrent requests), verify pod count scales up from 2 to 3+. 7. PDB test: attempt to drain a node with 2 equipment-catalog pods, verify at least 1 remains available. 8. CDN test: request product image via media.sigma1.com, verify Cloudflare cache HIT header on second request. 9. Grafana dashboard test: verify all service dashboards show data (non-empty panels for request rate, latency). 10. Verify 99.9% uptime target: all services have health checks responding, PDBs configured, HA database — calculate theoretical availability.

decision_points.question: Should the Cloudflare Tunnel connector (cloudflared) run as a Kubernetes Deployment within the cluster or as a standalone sidecar/DaemonSet? A Deployment is simpler but a DaemonSet offers node-level resilience.
decision_points.impact: Affects tunnel availability, resource usage, and failover behavior for all external API traffic.

decision_points.topic: R2 backup credentials and WAL archiving configuration
decision_points.question: What R2 bucket and access credentials should be used for PostgreSQL WAL archiving and base backups? Should a dedicated R2 bucket be created for backups separate from the media bucket?
options: Subfolder within existing sigma1-media R2 bucket
decision_points.impact: Affects backup isolation, lifecycle policies, access control, and cost tracking for database backups.

decision_points.topic: CDN custom domain and DNS configuration
decision_points.question: What is the confirmed custom domain for the R2 media CDN (e.g., media.sigma1.com)? Is the DNS zone already managed in Cloudflare, and are there existing records that could conflict?
decision_points.impact: Affects all frontend asset URLs, CORS configuration, and CDN cache rules.

subtasks.title: Scale PostgreSQL to 3-replica HA with automatic failover via CloudNative-PG
subtasks.description: Update the CloudNative-PG Cluster CR `sigma1-postgres` to `instances: 3` with automatic failover enabled. Configure PgBouncer connection pooling sidecar (built into CNPG) with appropriate pool sizes for production workloads.
subtasks.details:
1. Edit the existing CNPG Cluster CR YAML: set `spec.instances: 3`.
2. Enable PgBouncer pooler by adding `spec.managed.roles` and creating a CNPG `Pooler` CR targeting the cluster with `type: rw` for primary and `type: ro` for read replicas.
3. Set `spec.postgresql.parameters` for production: `shared_buffers: 256MB`, `max_connections: 200`, `wal_level: replica`.
4. Configure `spec.affinity` with pod anti-affinity rules to spread replicas across nodes.
5. Verify the cluster status shows 3 healthy instances with streaming replication active.
6. Update all service connection strings in the `sigma1-infra-endpoints` ConfigMap to point to the Pooler service rather than the raw cluster service.
subtasks.test_strategy: Verify `kubectl get cluster sigma1-postgres` shows 3 instances with status Ready. Kill the primary pod (`kubectl delete pod sigma1-postgres-1`), verify failover completes within 30 seconds by watching CNPG events. Confirm all services reconnect by checking their health endpoints return 200 after failover.

subtasks.title: Configure PostgreSQL WAL archiving and backup schedule to R2
subtasks.description: Set up continuous WAL archiving and scheduled base backups from the CNPG cluster to a Cloudflare R2 bucket for disaster recovery.
subtasks.details:
1. Create or confirm the R2 backup bucket (e.g., `sigma1-db-backups`) and generate S3-compatible access credentials.
2. Create a Kubernetes Secret containing the R2 endpoint URL, access key, and secret key for CNPG.
3. Update the CNPG Cluster CR with `spec.backup.barmanObjectStore` configuration pointing to R2 using the S3 protocol.
4. Set `spec.backup.barmanObjectStore.wal.compression: gzip` and `spec.backup.barmanObjectStore.wal.maxParallel: 2`.
5. Create a CNPG `ScheduledBackup` CR with `schedule: '0 2 * * *'` (daily at 2 AM) targeting the cluster.
6. Set `spec.backup.retentionPolicy: '14d'` for backup retention.
subtasks.test_strategy: Trigger a manual backup via `kubectl apply` of a one-off ScheduledBackup CR. Verify backup completes by checking CNPG backup status (`kubectl get backups`). Confirm WAL files appear in the R2 bucket via the Cloudflare dashboard or `rclone ls`. Verify the scheduled backup CR shows `lastScheduleTime` after the next scheduled run.

subtasks.title: Scale Valkey to sentinel mode with 3 replicas via OpsTree Redis Operator
subtasks.description: Update the OpsTree Redis CR to sentinel mode with 3 replicas, AOF persistence, allkeys-lru eviction, and appropriate memory limits for production.
subtasks.details:
1. Update the existing OpsTree Redis CR to use `spec.redisCluster` or `spec.redisSentinel` mode with 3 replicas.
2. Set `spec.kubernetesConfig.resources` with requests: 128Mi/250m and limits: 512Mi/500m per replica.
3. Configure Redis parameters: `maxmemory 384mb`, `maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru`, `appendonly yes`, `appendfsync everysec`.
4. Add pod anti-affinity to spread sentinel and Redis replicas across nodes.
5. Update the `sigma1-infra-endpoints` ConfigMap with the sentinel service endpoint so application services use sentinel-aware Redis clients.
6. Apply the updated CR and wait for all 3 replicas + sentinel pods to reach Ready status.
subtasks.test_strategy: Verify `kubectl get pods -l app=sigma1-valkey` shows 3 Redis replicas and sentinel pods all Running/Ready. Kill the master Redis pod, verify sentinel promotes a replica within 30 seconds. Exec into a service pod, run `redis-cli -h <sentinel-svc> SENTINEL get-master-addr-by-name mymaster` to confirm the new master is detected. Write a key and verify it replicates to remaining replicas.

subtasks.title: Configure Cloudflare Tunnel ingress routes for all API endpoints
subtasks.description: Deploy cloudflared in the cluster and configure tunnel ingress rules mapping all public API routes to their corresponding backend services.
subtasks.details:
1. Create a Cloudflare Tunnel via the Cloudflare dashboard or API, obtain the tunnel credentials JSON.
2. Store tunnel credentials as a Kubernetes Secret `cloudflare-tunnel-credentials` in the sigma1 namespace.
3. Create a ConfigMap `cloudflare-tunnel-config` with the ingress rules:
   - `/api/v1/catalog/*` → `http://equipment-catalog.sigma1.svc:8080`
   - `/api/v1/opportunities/*`, `/api/v1/projects/*`, `/api/v1/inventory/*`, `/api/v1/crew/*`, `/api/v1/deliveries/*` → `http://rms.sigma1.svc:8080`
   - `/api/v1/invoices/*`, `/api/v1/payments/*`, `/api/v1/finance/*`, `/api/v1/payroll/*`, `/api/v1/currency/*`, `/api/v1/vetting/*` → `http://finance-vetting.sigma1.svc:8080`
   - `/api/v1/stripe/webhook` → `http://finance-vetting.sigma1.svc:8080`
   - `/api/v1/social/*` → `http://social-engine.sigma1.svc:3000`
   - `/api/v1/chat/*` → `http://morgan.openclaw.svc:8080`
   - Default 404 catch-all rule.
4. Deploy a `cloudflared` Deployment (2 replicas for HA) mounting the credentials secret and config map.
5. Verify the tunnel appears as healthy in the Cloudflare dashboard.
subtasks.test_strategy: From an external network (outside the cluster), curl each route path against the tunnel's public hostname. Verify: `curl https://<domain>/api/v1/catalog/health` returns 200 from equipment-catalog; `curl https://<domain>/api/v1/social/health` returns 200 from social-engine; `curl https://<domain>/api/v1/chat/health` returns 200 from morgan. Verify the default catch-all returns 404 for unmatched paths. Check cloudflared pod logs for successful connection establishment.

subtasks.title: Configure Cloudflare CDN: R2 custom domain, cache rules, and security headers
subtasks.description: Set up R2 custom domain for media CDN, configure cache rules for different asset types, and add security headers via Cloudflare page/transform rules.
subtasks.details:
1. Configure R2 custom domain: in Cloudflare dashboard, link `media.sigma1.com` (or chosen domain) to the `sigma1-media` R2 bucket via R2 bucket settings → Custom Domains.
2. Create Cloudflare Cache Rules:
   - Product images (`/products/*`): Browser TTL 1 year, Edge TTL 1 year.
   - Social media photos (`/social/*`): Browser TTL 1 month, Edge TTL 1 month.
   - API responses (`/api/*`): respect origin Cache-Control headers, default no-cache.
3. Create Cloudflare Transform Rules for response headers on all routes:
   - `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload`
   - `X-Frame-Options: DENY`
   - `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`
   - `Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; img-src 'self' media.sigma1.com; ...` (adjust CSP per frontend needs).
   - `Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin`
4. Enable Cloudflare SSL/TLS mode to Full (Strict) for the domain.
5. Ensure CORS headers are set on the R2 custom domain for frontend access.
subtasks.test_strategy: Request a product image via `curl -I https://media.sigma1.com/products/test.jpg` — verify `cf-cache-status: HIT` on second request and `cache-control` header shows long TTL. Verify security headers are present on API responses: `curl -I https://<domain>/api/v1/catalog/health` should include HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options. Verify TLS 1.3 is negotiated via `curl -vvv https://<domain>/` and checking the TLS handshake output.

subtasks.title: Create Cilium network policy for equipment-catalog service
subtasks.description: Define a CiliumNetworkPolicy allowing equipment-catalog to reach only PostgreSQL, Valkey, and R2 (egress), and accept traffic only from the Cloudflare Tunnel ingress.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `CiliumNetworkPolicy` named `equipment-catalog-policy` in the sigma1 namespace.
2. Ingress rules: allow TCP 8080 from pods matching cloudflared labels and from other sigma1 services that legitimately call equipment-catalog (if any).
3. Egress rules:
   - Allow TCP 5432 to pods matching `sigma1-postgres` labels (PostgreSQL).
   - Allow TCP 6379 to pods matching `sigma1-valkey` labels (Valkey).
   - Allow HTTPS (TCP 443) egress to Cloudflare R2 endpoints (CIDR or FQDN-based).
   - Allow DNS (UDP 53, TCP 53) to kube-dns.
4. Deny all other ingress and egress by setting `spec.ingress` and `spec.egress` explicitly (implicit deny when policies exist).
5. Apply and verify with `kubectl get cnp equipment-catalog-policy`.
subtasks.test_strategy: Exec into an equipment-catalog pod: verify `curl http://sigma1-postgres:5432` connects (TCP), verify `redis-cli -h sigma1-valkey ping` returns PONG, verify `curl https://social-engine.sigma1.svc:3000` times out or is refused. Exec into a social-engine pod: verify it cannot reach equipment-catalog on port 8080 (unless explicitly allowed).

subtasks.title: Create Cilium network policy for RMS service
subtasks.description: Define a CiliumNetworkPolicy allowing rms to reach PostgreSQL, Valkey, finance-vetting (for vetting data), and Google Calendar API (egress), while blocking access to social-engine.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `CiliumNetworkPolicy` named `rms-policy` in the sigma1 namespace.
2. Ingress rules: allow TCP 8080 from cloudflared pods and from services that need to call RMS (e.g., finance-vetting for project data).
3. Egress rules:
   - Allow TCP 5432 to PostgreSQL pods.
   - Allow TCP 6379 to Valkey pods.
   - Allow TCP 8080 to finance-vetting pods (inter-service communication).
   - Allow HTTPS (TCP 443) egress to `*.googleapis.com` (Google Calendar API) via FQDN policy.
   - Allow DNS (UDP/TCP 53) to kube-dns.
4. Explicitly do NOT include social-engine in allowed egress.
5. Apply the policy.
subtasks.test_strategy: Exec into an rms pod: verify connectivity to PostgreSQL (TCP 5432), Valkey (TCP 6379), finance-vetting (TCP 8080), and `curl https://www.googleapis.com` succeeds. Verify `curl http://social-engine.sigma1.svc:3000` is blocked (timeout or connection refused).

subtasks.title: Create Cilium network policy for finance-vetting service
subtasks.description: Define a CiliumNetworkPolicy allowing finance-vetting to reach PostgreSQL, Valkey, Stripe API, external vetting APIs (OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, Google, Credit), and RMS, while blocking direct access to social-engine.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `CiliumNetworkPolicy` named `finance-vetting-policy` in the sigma1 namespace.
2. Ingress rules: allow TCP 8080 from cloudflared pods and from rms pods.
3. Egress rules:
   - Allow TCP 5432 to PostgreSQL pods.
   - Allow TCP 6379 to Valkey pods.
   - Allow TCP 8080 to rms pods (for project data lookups).
   - Allow HTTPS (TCP 443) egress to: `api.stripe.com`, `api.opencorporates.com`, `api.linkedin.com`, `*.googleapis.com`, and credit check API domains via FQDN-based policies.
   - Allow DNS (UDP/TCP 53) to kube-dns.
4. Do NOT include social-engine in allowed destinations.
5. Apply the policy.
subtasks.test_strategy: Exec into a finance-vetting pod: verify connectivity to PostgreSQL, Valkey, rms (TCP 8080), `curl https://api.stripe.com` succeeds. Verify `curl http://social-engine.sigma1.svc:3000` is blocked. Verify `curl https://api.opencorporates.com` succeeds.

subtasks.title: Create Cilium network policy for social-engine service
subtasks.description: Define a CiliumNetworkPolicy allowing social-engine to reach PostgreSQL, Valkey, R2, and social media/AI APIs (Instagram, LinkedIn, Facebook, TikTok, OpenAI) via egress.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `CiliumNetworkPolicy` named `social-engine-policy` in the sigma1 namespace.
2. Ingress rules: allow TCP 3000 from cloudflared pods and from morgan pods.
3. Egress rules:
   - Allow TCP 5432 to PostgreSQL pods.
   - Allow TCP 6379 to Valkey pods.
   - Allow HTTPS (TCP 443) to Cloudflare R2 endpoints.
   - Allow HTTPS (TCP 443) to: `graph.facebook.com`, `graph.instagram.com`, `api.linkedin.com`, `open.tiktokapis.com`, `api.openai.com` via FQDN policies.
   - Allow DNS (UDP/TCP 53) to kube-dns.
4. Apply the policy.
subtasks.test_strategy: Exec into a social-engine pod: verify connectivity to PostgreSQL, Valkey, R2 endpoint, and `curl https://api.openai.com` succeeds. Verify `curl http://rms.sigma1.svc:8080` is blocked. Verify `curl http://finance-vetting.sigma1.svc:8080` is blocked.

subtasks.title: Create Cilium network policy for morgan service
subtasks.description: Define a CiliumNetworkPolicy allowing morgan to reach all sigma1 services, Signal servers, ElevenLabs, and Twilio via egress.
subtasks.details:
1. Create `CiliumNetworkPolicy` named `morgan-policy` in the openclaw namespace.
2. Ingress rules: allow TCP 8080 from cloudflared pods.
3. Egress rules:
   - Allow TCP 8080 to equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting pods in sigma1 namespace.
   - Allow TCP 3000 to social-engine pods in sigma1 namespace.
   - Allow TCP 5432 to PostgreSQL pods (if morgan has direct DB access).
   - Allow TCP 6379 to Valkey pods.
   - Allow HTTPS (TCP 443) to: Signal servers (`chat.signal.org`, `storage.signal.org`), `api.elevenlabs.io`, `api.twilio.com` via FQDN policies.
   - Allow DNS (UDP/TCP 53) to kube-dns.
4. Apply the policy.
subtasks.test_strategy: Exec into a morgan pod: verify connectivity to all sigma1 services (equipment-catalog:8080, rms:8080, finance-vetting:8080, social-engine:3000). Verify `curl https://api.elevenlabs.io` and `curl https://api.twilio.com` succeed. Verify the pod cannot reach arbitrary external IPs not in the allowed list.

subtasks.title: Apply default-deny Cilium network policy for sigma1 and openclaw namespaces
subtasks.description: Create default-deny CiliumNetworkPolicies for both namespaces to ensure any pod without an explicit policy has no network access.
subtasks.details:
1. Create a `CiliumNetworkPolicy` named `default-deny-sigma1` in the sigma1 namespace with empty `spec.ingress: []` and `spec.egress: []` matching all pods (`spec.endpointSelector: {}`).
2. Create a `CiliumNetworkPolicy` named `default-deny-openclaw` in the openclaw namespace with the same default-deny pattern.
3. Note: This must be applied AFTER all service-specific policies are in place to avoid disrupting running services.
4. Verify no service disruption after applying by checking health endpoints of all services.
subtasks.test_strategy: Deploy a test pod (e.g., `busybox`) in the sigma1 namespace without any matching policy labels. Verify it cannot reach any service or external endpoint. Verify all existing services still pass their health checks after the default-deny is applied. Remove the test pod.

subtasks.title: Configure production resource limits for all services
subtasks.description: Update Deployment manifests for all 5 services with production-tuned resource requests and limits, and set replica counts to production values.
subtasks.details:
1. Update equipment-catalog Deployment: `resources.requests: {memory: 256Mi, cpu: 500m}`, `resources.limits: {memory: 1Gi, cpu: '2000m'}`, `replicas: 2`.
2. Update rms Deployment: same as equipment-catalog — requests 256Mi/500m, limits 1Gi/2000m, replicas 2.
3. Update finance-vetting Deployment: same as above — requests 256Mi/500m, limits 1Gi/2000m, replicas 2.
4. Update social-engine Deployment: `resources.requests: {memory: 256Mi, cpu: 250m}`, `resources.limits: {memory: 512Mi, cpu: '1000m'}`, `replicas: 2`.
5. Update morgan Deployment: `resources.requests: {memory: 512Mi, cpu: '1000m'}`, `resources.limits: {memory: 2Gi, cpu: '2000m'}`, `replicas: 1` (stateful, no scaling).
6. Apply all updated Deployments and verify pods restart with new resource settings.
subtasks.test_strategy: For each service, run `kubectl describe pod <pod>` and verify the resource requests and limits match the specified values. Verify `kubectl get deployment <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}'` returns the correct replica count. Confirm no pods are in OOMKilled or CrashLoopBackOff state after applying new limits.

subtasks.title: Create HorizontalPodAutoscalers for stateless services
subtasks.description: Deploy HPA resources for equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, and social-engine with min 2, max 5 replicas, targeting 70% CPU utilization.
subtasks.details:
1. Create HPA for equipment-catalog: `apiVersion: autoscaling/v2`, `minReplicas: 2`, `maxReplicas: 5`, `metrics: [{type: Resource, resource: {name: cpu, target: {type: Utilization, averageUtilization: 70}}}]`.
2. Create HPA for rms with identical settings.
3. Create HPA for finance-vetting with identical settings.
4. Create HPA for social-engine with identical settings.
5. Verify metrics-server is running in the cluster (required for HPA CPU metrics).
6. Apply all 4 HPA manifests and verify they show `TARGETS` as non-`<unknown>`.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `kubectl get hpa` and verify all 4 HPAs show current CPU metrics (not `<unknown>/70%`). Generate load on equipment-catalog using `kubectl run loadtest --image=busybox -- sh -c 'while true; do wget -q -O- http://equipment-catalog:8080/health; done'` and verify the HPA scales pods from 2 to 3+ within 2-3 minutes. Remove the load generator and verify scale-down occurs within 5 minutes.

subtasks.title: Create PodDisruptionBudgets for all multi-replica services
subtasks.description: Deploy PDB resources for all services with 2+ replicas ensuring at least 1 pod remains available during voluntary disruptions.
subtasks.details:
1. Create PDB for equipment-catalog: `spec.minAvailable: 1`, `spec.selector.matchLabels: {app: equipment-catalog}`.
2. Create PDB for rms: `spec.minAvailable: 1`, matching rms labels.
3. Create PDB for finance-vetting: `spec.minAvailable: 1`, matching finance-vetting labels.
4. Create PDB for social-engine: `spec.minAvailable: 1`, matching social-engine labels.
5. Note: morgan is single-replica and stateful, so no PDB (disruption would always violate minAvailable).
6. Apply all 4 PDB manifests.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run `kubectl get pdb` and verify all 4 PDBs show `ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS: 1` (since each has 2 replicas and minAvailable 1). Attempt `kubectl drain <node>` on a node running 2 equipment-catalog pods — verify the drain respects the PDB and keeps at least 1 pod running (drain may block until a pod is rescheduled elsewhere).

subtasks.title: Configure Prometheus scraping for CNPG PostgreSQL metrics and alert rules
subtasks.description: Set up Prometheus ServiceMonitor for CNPG metrics and create PrometheusRule CRs for critical database alerts (replication lag, connection pool exhaustion).
subtasks.details:
1. Create a `ServiceMonitor` CR targeting the CNPG metrics service (CNPG exposes metrics on port 9187 via the built-in Prometheus exporter).
2. Verify the ServiceMonitor label selectors match the Prometheus operator's `serviceMonitorSelector`.
3. Create a `PrometheusRule` CR with alert rules:
   - `CNPGReplicationLag`: `cnpg_pg_replication_lag > 10` for 2 minutes → severity warning.
   - `CNPGReplicationLagCritical`: `cnpg_pg_replication_lag > 30` for 1 minute → severity critical.
   - `CNPGConnectionPoolExhaustion`: `cnpg_pgbouncer_pools_server_active / cnpg_pgbouncer_pools_server_max > 0.9` for 5 minutes → severity warning.
   - `CNPGInstanceDown`: `cnpg_collector_up == 0` for 1 minute → severity critical.
4. Apply both CRs and verify they appear in Prometheus targets and rules UI.
subtasks.test_strategy: Access the Prometheus web UI, navigate to Targets, and verify the CNPG target is listed and in UP state. Navigate to Rules, verify all 4 alert rules are loaded and in `inactive` state (no current alerts). Query `cnpg_pg_replication_lag` in Prometheus and verify it returns data for all replicas. Temporarily pause replication on a replica (if possible in test) and verify the alert fires.

subtasks.title: Create Grafana dashboards for all services, PostgreSQL, and Valkey metrics
subtasks.description: Import or create Grafana dashboards showing request rate, error rate, latency percentiles for each service, plus dedicated dashboards for PostgreSQL and Valkey metrics.
subtasks.details:
1. Create a Grafana dashboard for each backend service (equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, social-engine, morgan) with panels:
   - Request rate (requests/sec) from Prometheus metrics.
   - Error rate (5xx responses/sec).
   - Latency p50, p95, p99 (from histogram metrics).
   - Pod CPU and memory usage.
2. Create a PostgreSQL dashboard with panels:
   - Active connections vs max connections.
   - Replication lag per replica.
   - Transactions per second.
   - PgBouncer pool utilization.
   - Disk usage.
3. Create a Valkey dashboard with panels:
   - Connected clients.
   - Memory usage vs maxmemory.
   - Hit rate vs miss rate.
   - Evicted keys/sec.
   - Sentinel status.
4. Store all dashboards as ConfigMaps with the `grafana_dashboard: '1'` label for automatic Grafana sidecar discovery.
5. Apply ConfigMaps and verify dashboards appear in Grafana.
subtasks.test_strategy: Open Grafana UI and navigate to each dashboard. Verify all 7 dashboards are listed (5 services + PostgreSQL + Valkey). For each dashboard, verify panels are not showing 'No data' — at minimum, CPU/memory panels should have data from running pods. Send a few test requests to equipment-catalog and verify the request rate panel updates within 30 seconds.

subtasks.title: Configure Loki log aggregation labels for all service pods
subtasks.description: Ensure all service pod templates include correct labels and annotations for Loki log collection, and verify logs are queryable in Grafana.
subtasks.details:
1. Verify Loki and Promtail (or Grafana Agent) are deployed in the cluster and collecting logs.
2. For each service Deployment (equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, social-engine, morgan), ensure pod template labels include: `app`, `service`, `namespace`, and `environment: production`.
3. If using Promtail, verify the DaemonSet config includes the sigma1 and openclaw namespaces in its scrape targets.
4. If using Grafana Agent, verify the Agent config's `loki.source.kubernetes` includes the correct namespace selectors.
5. Verify each service outputs logs in a parseable format (JSON preferred) so Loki can extract fields.
6. Add a Grafana data source for Loki if not already configured.
7. Create a simple Loki-based panel in each service's Grafana dashboard showing recent log lines filtered by `{app="<service-name>"}`.
subtasks.test_strategy: In Grafana Explore, select the Loki data source and query `{namespace="sigma1"}` — verify logs appear from all sigma1 services. Query `{app="equipment-catalog"}` and verify logs are specific to that service. Query `{namespace="openclaw", app="morgan"}` and verify morgan logs appear. Verify no logs are missing by comparing `kubectl logs <pod>` output with Loki query results.

title: Production Hardening: RBAC Middleware, Secret Rotation, Audit Logging (Bolt - Kubernetes/Helm)
description: Finalize production security posture: configure External Secrets rotation policies for all API keys, implement audit logging pipeline (all services → audit schema via NATS fire-and-forget), configure Kubernetes RBAC for least-privilege service accounts, set up Prometheus alerting rules for security events, and verify end-to-end GDPR orchestration. Ensure the admin allowlist middleware is consistently deployed across all services.
details:
1. External Secrets rotation policies:
   - Stripe API key: rotation period 90 days, notification alert 14 days before expiry.
   - OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, Google, Credit API keys: rotation period 180 days.
   - ElevenLabs, Twilio: rotation period 90 days.
   - OpenAI API key: rotation period 90 days.
   - Cloudflare R2 credentials: rotation period 180 days.
   - Google Calendar service account: rotation period 365 days.
   - PostgreSQL passwords: rotation period 90 days (CNPG supports automated rotation).
   - Configure ExternalSecret refresh intervals to match rotation periods.
2. Kubernetes RBAC — least-privilege service accounts:
   - Each service Deployment gets its own ServiceAccount (e.g., `sa-equipment-catalog`, `sa-rms`, `sa-finance-vetting`, `sa-social-engine`, `sa-morgan`).
   - ServiceAccount RBAC: only read access to own secrets and ConfigMaps. No cluster-wide permissions.
   - Morgan ServiceAccount: additional read access to all sigma1 namespace services (for health checking).
   - Disable automountServiceAccountToken for pods that don't need Kubernetes API access.
3. Audit logging pipeline:
   - All services publish audit events to NATS `sigma1.audit.{service}.*` (fire-and-forget per D3).
   - Deploy a lightweight audit-logger subscriber (Go binary or NATS JetStream consumer) that:
     a. Subscribes to `sigma1.audit.>`.
     b. Writes events to `audit.events` table in PostgreSQL (event_type, service, actor, resource_id, action, metadata JSONB, timestamp).
   - Audit events include: customer data access, admin actions, GDPR deletions, invoice creation, opportunity conversion, social media publishing.
   - Retention policy: audit logs retained for 7 years (GDPR compliance).
4. Prometheus alerting rules:
   - High error rate: >5% 5xx responses in 5 minutes → PagerDuty/Slack alert.
   - Service down: health check failing for >1 minute → critical alert.
   - PostgreSQL replication lag >30s → warning alert.
   - Valkey memory >80% → warning alert.
   - Certificate expiry <14 days → warning alert.
   - GDPR deletion failure → critical alert.
   - Unusual API access patterns (>10x normal request rate) → warning alert.
5. Verify admin allowlist consistency:
   - Audit all services to confirm admin-only endpoints check X-Service-Identity/X-User-ID headers.
   - Verify allowlist values (morgan-service, mike-user-id) are consistent across all services via shared ConfigMap or environment variable.
   - Document the middleware pattern for future RBAC swap (per D11).
6. GDPR end-to-end verification:
   - Create test customer data across all services.
   - Trigger GDPR orchestrator.
   - Verify all data deleted from all schemas (public, rms, finance, vetting, social).
   - Verify audit log entry created in `audit.gdpr_deletion_log`.
   - Verify the process completes within 60 seconds for a single customer.
7. Security scanning baseline:
   - Run Semgrep OWASP rules against all codebases, ensure zero critical/high findings.
   - Run cargo-audit on Rust services, govulncheck on Go service, npm audit on Node.js service.
   - Document all accepted low/medium findings with justification.
8. Deploy audit-logger subscriber as Kubernetes Deployment in `sigma1` namespace (1 replica, minimal resources).
test_strategy: 1. Secret rotation test: manually rotate a test secret in External Secrets backend, verify pods pick up new value within the configured refresh interval without restart. 2. RBAC test: exec into equipment-catalog pod, verify `kubectl get secrets` fails with forbidden (ServiceAccount has no cluster permissions). 3. Audit logging test: trigger a product creation via Equipment Catalog API, verify audit event appears in `audit.events` table within 5 seconds with correct service, actor, and action. 4. Prometheus alert test: stop a service health endpoint, verify alert fires within 2 minutes in Alertmanager. 5. Admin allowlist consistency test: send admin request to each service (equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, social-engine) with correct X-Service-Identity header → 200; without → 403. All 4 services behave identically. 6. GDPR end-to-end test: create customer with data in all services, run orchestrator, verify zero records remain for that customer_id across all schemas; verify audit.gdpr_deletion_log has completion entry. 7. Security scan test: run Semgrep against all repos, confirm zero critical/high findings. 8. Audit log retention: verify audit.events table has no automatic deletion (retention handled by policy, not TTL). 9. Verify audit-logger subscriber processes >100 events/second without backpressure (load test). 10. Verify all service Deployments reference their dedicated ServiceAccount (not default).

options: Lightweight Go binary subscribing to NATS
NATS JetStream consumer with durable subscription
decision_points.context: The details mention either a Go binary or a NATS JetStream consumer. A Go binary gives full control over batching and error handling. A JetStream durable consumer provides built-in at-least-once delivery and replay. Since audit logs require 7-year GDPR retention with zero data loss, the delivery guarantee matters. Need to decide before implementing subtask 10004.

options: PagerDuty for critical + Slack for warnings
decision_points.context: The details reference PagerDuty/Slack but the actual accounts/integrations and routing preferences need confirmation. This affects Alertmanager ConfigMap configuration in subtask 10005.

subtasks.title: Configure External Secrets rotation policies for 90-day secrets (Stripe, ElevenLabs, Twilio, OpenAI, PostgreSQL)
subtasks.description: Add rotation period annotations and refresh intervals to all ExternalSecret CRs that require 90-day rotation cycles. Configure expiry notification alerts at 14 days before rotation deadline.
subtasks.details:
1. For each 90-day secret (Stripe API key, ElevenLabs API key, Twilio credentials, OpenAI API key, PostgreSQL passwords), update the corresponding ExternalSecret CR in the sigma1 namespace.
2. Set `spec.refreshInterval` to an appropriate value (e.g., `1h`) so pods detect rotated values promptly.
3. For Stripe: add rotation period 90 days with a 14-day pre-expiry notification annotation.
4. For PostgreSQL passwords: verify CNPG automated rotation is enabled and the ExternalSecret refresh interval aligns with CNPG's rotation schedule (90 days).
5. For ElevenLabs, Twilio, OpenAI: set rotation period 90 days in the secrets backend (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager or Vault rotation policy).
6. Ensure all ExternalSecret CRs have `spec.target.deletionPolicy: Retain` so secrets aren't lost during rotation.
7. Add Helm values or Kustomize overlays so rotation periods are configurable per environment.
subtasks.test_strategy: Manually rotate a test Stripe secret in the secrets backend. Verify the ExternalSecret controller picks up the new value within the configured refreshInterval. Verify the pod environment variable reflects the new secret without pod restart. Check that pre-expiry notification metadata is present on the ExternalSecret status.

subtasks.title: Configure External Secrets rotation policies for 180-day and 365-day secrets (OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, Google, Credit API, R2, Google Calendar)
subtasks.description: Add rotation period annotations and refresh intervals to all ExternalSecret CRs that require 180-day or 365-day rotation cycles.
subtasks.details:
1. For each 180-day secret (OpenCorporates API key, LinkedIn API key, Google API key, Credit API key, Cloudflare R2 credentials), update the corresponding ExternalSecret CR.
2. Set `spec.refreshInterval` appropriately (e.g., `6h` for 180-day secrets since urgency is lower).
3. For Google Calendar service account: set rotation period to 365 days with a 30-day pre-expiry notification.
4. Configure rotation policies in the secrets backend for each key.
5. Verify all ExternalSecret CRs reference the correct secret store and key paths.
6. Add Helm values or Kustomize overlays for rotation period configurability.
subtasks.test_strategy: Verify each ExternalSecret CR has the correct refreshInterval and rotation annotations by running `kubectl get externalsecret -n sigma1 -o yaml` and confirming rotation metadata. Manually rotate an R2 credential and verify the new value propagates within the refresh interval.

subtasks.title: Create per-service ServiceAccounts with least-privilege RBAC Roles and RoleBindings
subtasks.description: Define dedicated ServiceAccounts for each service Deployment (equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, social-engine, morgan) with Roles scoped to only their own secrets and ConfigMaps. Disable automountServiceAccountToken where Kubernetes API access is not needed.
subtasks.details:
1. Create ServiceAccount manifests: `sa-equipment-catalog`, `sa-rms`, `sa-finance-vetting`, `sa-social-engine`, `sa-morgan` in the sigma1 namespace.
2. For each non-Morgan service, create a Role that grants only `get`, `list`, `watch` on Secrets and ConfigMaps matching that service's naming convention (e.g., `equipment-catalog-*`).
3. Create corresponding RoleBindings binding each Role to its ServiceAccount.
4. For Morgan's ServiceAccount: create an additional Role granting read access to all services' endpoints/health resources in the sigma1 namespace (for health checking).
5. Update each Deployment spec to reference its dedicated ServiceAccount via `spec.template.spec.serviceAccountName`.
6. Set `automountServiceAccountToken: false` on all Deployments that do not need Kubernetes API access (equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, social-engine). Morgan needs it for health checking.
7. Remove any usage of the `default` ServiceAccount from all Deployments.
8. Package all RBAC manifests in the Helm chart under `templates/rbac/`.
subtasks.test_strategy: 1. `kubectl auth can-i get secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:sigma1:sa-equipment-catalog -n sigma1` → yes for own secrets. 2. `kubectl auth can-i get secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:sigma1:sa-equipment-catalog -n kube-system` → no. 3. Exec into equipment-catalog pod, run `kubectl get secrets` → forbidden. 4. Verify Morgan SA can read service endpoints. 5. Verify all Deployments reference their dedicated SA, not `default`.

subtasks.title: Deploy audit-logger subscriber (NATS consumer → PostgreSQL audit.events table)
subtasks.description: Create and deploy a lightweight audit-logger service that subscribes to NATS `sigma1.audit.>` wildcard subject and persists all audit events to the `audit.events` PostgreSQL table with the defined schema.
subtasks.details:
1. Create the `audit.events` table migration:
   ```sql
   CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS audit;
   CREATE TABLE audit.events (
     id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
     event_type TEXT NOT NULL,
     service TEXT NOT NULL,
     actor TEXT NOT NULL,
     resource_id TEXT,
     action TEXT NOT NULL,
     metadata JSONB DEFAULT '{}',
     timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
   );
   CREATE INDEX idx_audit_events_service ON audit.events(service);
   CREATE INDEX idx_audit_events_timestamp ON audit.events(timestamp);
   CREATE INDEX idx_audit_events_actor ON audit.events(actor);
   ```
2. Ensure the `audit.gdpr_deletion_log` table also exists (may already be created by GDPR orchestrator migration).
3. Set retention policy: no automatic TTL deletion — 7-year retention enforced by policy. Add a comment on the table documenting the retention requirement.
4. Create the audit-logger Kubernetes Deployment manifest:
   - Image: lightweight Go binary or NATS JetStream consumer (per decision point).
   - 1 replica, resource requests: 64Mi memory, 50m CPU; limits: 128Mi memory, 100m CPU.
   - Environment variables: NATS_URL, PG_DSN from sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap and secrets.
   - Subscribe to `sigma1.audit.>` wildcard.
   - Batch insert events (e.g., flush every 100 events or 1 second, whichever comes first).
5. Add health check endpoint on the audit-logger for liveness/readiness probes.
6. Create a dedicated ServiceAccount `sa-audit-logger` with read access to its own secrets only.
7. Deploy in sigma1 namespace.
subtasks.test_strategy: 1. Publish a test audit event to NATS `sigma1.audit.test.created`. 2. Verify the event appears in `audit.events` table within 5 seconds. 3. Verify all fields (event_type, service, actor, resource_id, action, metadata, timestamp) are correctly populated. 4. Load test: publish 100+ events/second, verify no backpressure or message loss. 5. Verify audit-logger pod is healthy via readiness probe.

subtasks.title: Define Prometheus alerting rules and Alertmanager routing configuration
subtasks.description: Create PrometheusRule CRs for all 7 alert categories (error rate, service down, replication lag, Valkey memory, cert expiry, GDPR failure, anomalous access) and configure Alertmanager routing to appropriate channels.
subtasks.details:
1. Create a PrometheusRule CR in sigma1 namespace with the following rules:
   a. **High error rate**: `rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]) / rate(http_requests_total[5m]) > 0.05` → severity: critical, route to PagerDuty/Slack.
   b. **Service down**: `up{namespace="sigma1"} == 0` for >1 minute → severity: critical.
   c. **PostgreSQL replication lag**: `pg_replication_lag_seconds > 30` → severity: warning.
   d. **Valkey memory**: `redis_memory_used_bytes / redis_memory_max_bytes > 0.8` → severity: warning.
   e. **Certificate expiry**: `certmanager_certificate_expiration_timestamp_seconds - time() < 14*24*3600` → severity: warning.
   f. **GDPR deletion failure**: custom metric from GDPR orchestrator indicating failure → severity: critical.
   g. **Anomalous access**: `rate(http_requests_total{namespace="sigma1"}[5m]) > 10 * avg_over_time(rate(http_requests_total{namespace="sigma1"}[5m])[1d:5m])` → severity: warning.
2. Configure Alertmanager ConfigMap/Secret with routing:
   - Critical alerts → primary notification channel (per decision point).
   - Warning alerts → secondary channel.
   - Group by: namespace, service, alertname.
   - Group wait: 30s, group interval: 5m, repeat interval: 4h.
3. Add inhibition rules: if service is down, suppress error rate alerts for that service.
4. Package as Helm template.
subtasks.test_strategy: 1. Stop a service's health endpoint, verify `ServiceDown` alert fires in Alertmanager within 2 minutes. 2. Verify all 7 PrometheusRule expressions are syntactically valid by checking Prometheus /rules endpoint. 3. Verify Alertmanager routing by triggering a test alert and confirming it reaches the configured channel. 4. Verify inhibition: when service down alert fires, error rate alert for same service is suppressed.

subtasks.title: Audit and enforce admin allowlist middleware consistency across all services
subtasks.description: Verify that all services implement the admin allowlist middleware identically, checking X-Service-Identity and X-User-ID headers against a shared ConfigMap, and document the middleware pattern for future RBAC migration.
subtasks.details:
1. Create or update the shared ConfigMap `sigma1-admin-allowlist` containing the allowed values: `morgan-service` for X-Service-Identity and `mike-user-id` for X-User-ID.
2. Audit each service's admin endpoint middleware:
   - equipment-catalog: verify admin routes check headers.
   - rms: verify admin routes check headers.
   - finance-vetting: verify admin routes check headers.
   - social-engine: verify admin routes check headers.
3. For any service missing or inconsistently implementing the check, file a fix (update the service's middleware to read from the shared ConfigMap).
4. Ensure all services reference the allowlist via `envFrom` from the shared ConfigMap, not hardcoded values.
5. Document the middleware pattern in a `docs/security/admin-allowlist.md` file:
   - How the middleware works.
   - Which headers are checked.
   - How to add new admin identities.
   - Migration path to full RBAC (per D11 decision).
subtasks.test_strategy: Send admin requests to each service (equipment-catalog, rms, finance-vetting, social-engine) with correct X-Service-Identity header → 200. Send without header → 403. Send with incorrect header → 403. All 4 services behave identically. Verify allowlist values come from ConfigMap, not hardcoded.

subtasks.title: Run GDPR end-to-end orchestration verification test
subtasks.description: Execute a full GDPR deletion workflow: create test customer data across all service schemas, trigger the GDPR orchestrator, and verify complete data removal and audit log creation within 60 seconds.
subtasks.details:
1. Create a test customer with data in all schemas:
   - public schema: customer record in equipment-catalog.
   - rms schema: rental/opportunity records.
   - finance schema: invoice and payment records.
   - vetting schema: vetting/credit check records.
   - social schema: social media content records.
2. Record the customer_id.
3. Trigger the GDPR deletion orchestrator for that customer_id.
4. Verify data removal:
   - Query each schema for the customer_id → zero rows.
   - Check `audit.gdpr_deletion_log` for a completion entry with the customer_id.
5. Measure total orchestration time → must complete within 60 seconds.
6. Verify audit events were published to NATS and persisted in `audit.events` table with action `gdpr_deletion`.
7. Document the test as a repeatable script (shell or test file) for CI inclusion.
subtasks.test_strategy: Run the GDPR orchestrator test script. Verify: (a) zero records remain for the test customer_id across all 5 schemas, (b) audit.gdpr_deletion_log has a completion entry, (c) audit.events has gdpr_deletion events from each service, (d) total time < 60 seconds. Run test 3 times to confirm consistency.

subtasks.title: Run security scanning baseline (Semgrep, cargo-audit, govulncheck, npm audit)
subtasks.description: Execute security vulnerability scanners against all codebases, ensure zero critical/high findings, and document all accepted low/medium findings with justification.
subtasks.details:
1. Run Semgrep with OWASP ruleset against all service codebases:
   - equipment-catalog (Rust)
   - rms (Rust)
   - finance-vetting (Rust)
   - social-engine (Rust)
   - morgan (TypeScript/Bun)
   - audit-logger (Go)
2. Run `cargo audit` on all Rust services.
3. Run `govulncheck` on the Go audit-logger service.
4. Run `npm audit` (or `bun audit` equivalent) on the Morgan TypeScript service.
5. Triage results:
   - Critical/High findings: must be resolved before production. File issues or fix inline.
   - Medium/Low findings: document each with justification for acceptance in `docs/security/scan-baseline.md`.
6. Save scan output artifacts for compliance records.
7. Create a CI script that runs these scans so they can be integrated into the pipeline later.
subtasks.test_strategy: Verify Semgrep reports zero critical/high findings across all codebases. Verify cargo-audit reports zero known vulnerabilities (or all are documented as accepted). Verify govulncheck and npm audit similarly. Verify docs/security/scan-baseline.md exists with documented findings.
